turns.content: If p < 0.05 doesn't mean there's a 95% chance the hypothesis is true, what does it actually mean?

It means: assuming the null hypothesis is true (and the rest of the model assumptions are true), the probability of obtaining a test statistic at least as extreme as the one you observed is less than 0.05. More concretely: - You pick a test (t-test, z-test, chi-square, etc.) and a test statistic that measures discrepancy between data and what the null predicts. - You compute that statistic from your data. - The p-value is the tail-area probability, under the null distribution of that statistic, of seeing a value as extreme or more extreme than what you got. So p < 0.05 says “this result would be somewhat rare if the null were true,” not “the alternative is probably true.”
“As extreme or more extreme” always confuses me. Extreme in which direction? Like if my effect is positive, do we only look at the positive tail?

“Extreme” is defined by the alternative hypothesis and the test you chose. - One-sided (one-tailed) test: “extreme” is only in the direction specified by the alternative. If your alternative is “effect > 0,” then you look only at the upper tail of the null distribution. - Two-sided (two-tailed) test: “extreme” means “far from the null value in either direction.” You look at both tails and add them (or equivalently compute a two-sided tail probability). Example in words: Suppose the null says a mean difference is 0. If you observe a difference of +2 units: - One-sided p-value for “difference > 0” is P(T ≥ +2 | H0). - Two-sided p-value is P(|T| ≥ 2 | H0) = P(T ≥ +2 or T ≤ −2 | H0). The phrase “as extreme or more extreme” is basically “at least as incompatible with the null as what I saw,” where “incompatible” is measured by the test statistic.
But if I didn’t decide one-sided or two-sided before, can I just choose after seeing the sign?

You technically can compute either, but choosing after seeing the data changes the meaning. If you pick the tail after looking at the sign, you’re using the data twice: once to choose the direction and once to test it. That inflates the false positive rate. If you didn’t pre-specify direction and you’re open to either sign, the honest default is the two-sided test. If you had a strong directional claim independent of the data, then a one-sided test can be appropriate, but it should be decided in advance.
Okay. Still, I’m stuck on the intuition. If p = 0.03, what can I say in plain language without being wrong?

A careful plain-language statement is something like: “If the null hypothesis were true, results this extreme or more extreme would occur about 3% of the time (under repeated sampling and the test’s assumptions). Therefore, the data are relatively inconsistent with the null.” You can also say: - “The result is statistically significant at the 0.05 level” (if you used 0.05 as your threshold). But avoid: - “There’s a 97% chance the alternative is true.” - “There’s a 3% chance the null is true.” Those are different probabilities (posterior probabilities) that require additional information (like priors in a Bayesian framework).
What do you mean by “under repeated sampling”? I only did it once.

Frequentist p-values are defined in terms of a hypothetical repetition process. Imagine you could repeat the exact same procedure many times: - same design, - same sample size, - same measurement process, - same analysis plan, - and the null is actually true in the data-generating mechanism. You’d get a distribution of possible test statistics across repetitions. The p-value is the fraction of those repetitions where the statistic is at least as extreme as what you observed. So even though you only ran the procedure once, the probability statement is about what would happen across repeated runs under the null.
So the p-value is a probability about my data, not about the hypothesis?

Close: it’s a probability about the test statistic (a function of the data) under the assumption that the null hypothesis is true. It is not a probability distribution over hypotheses. One way to phrase it: - p-value = P(data as or more incompatible with H0 than observed | H0). The thing you might want (but p-value isn’t) is: - P(H0 | data) or P(H1 | data). Those reverse the conditioning and generally require a different framework.
Is that the “prosecutor’s fallacy” thing? Like mixing up P(A|B) and P(B|A)?

Yes, it’s the same underlying logical error: confusing P(data | hypothesis) with P(hypothesis | data). A p-value is a kind of P(data | H0) summary (not exactly the full likelihood, but related). People misread it as P(H0 | data). Those are not the same and can differ drastically. Bayes’ theorem connects them: P(H0 | data) ∝ P(data | H0) * P(H0), and similarly for H1. Without priors and a model for H1, you can’t get P(H0 | data).
But why do people use 0.05 then? It sounds arbitrary.

It’s largely conventional. Historically, 0.05 became a common default threshold, popularized in early statistical practice. Conceptually, choosing α = 0.05 means: - You’re willing to tolerate a 5% Type I error rate in the long run, meaning if H0 is true and you repeat the procedure many times, about 5% of tests will incorrectly reject H0. But there’s nothing magical about 0.05. Depending on consequences and context, people might choose 0.10, 0.01, 0.001, or adjust for multiple comparisons, etc.
Wait, you said Type I error is about rejecting a true null. So p < 0.05 means my Type I error is 5%?

Not exactly. α = 0.05 is the pre-chosen rule “reject if p ≤ 0.05.” Under the null, that rule has a 5% long-run false positive rate (approximately, under the assumptions). But your observed p-value (say 0.03) is not “your probability of a Type I error.” The p-value is a continuous measure of how extreme your data are under H0; α is a fixed decision threshold. The Type I error rate is a property of the testing procedure, not a probability attached to your specific outcome in a single experiment.
That sounds subtle. So if p = 0.03 and α = 0.05, I reject. But what does 0.03 add beyond just “reject”?

The p-value gives a graded measure of how incompatible the data are with H0, within that test. Two results can both be “significant at 0.05” but with different p-values, indicating different degrees of extremeness under H0. However, p-values are not a direct measure of effect size, importance, or probability the hypothesis is true. Also, the mapping from p-values to evidence isn’t linear; p=0.049 and p=0.051 are practically similar but lead to different binary decisions if you threshold at 0.05.
Is a smaller p-value always stronger evidence?

Within a fixed testing framework and assuming all assumptions hold, smaller p-values typically correspond to data that are less likely under H0, so they are often treated as “more evidence against H0.” But there are caveats: - With huge sample sizes, tiny effects can yield extremely small p-values. - With violated assumptions or flexible analysis choices, p-values can be misleading. - A p-value doesn’t compare H0 to a specific alternative; it only measures incompatibility with H0. So smaller p-values can be “more surprising under H0,” but that doesn’t automatically mean “more practically important” or “more likely that some specific alternative is true.”
Can you show me a numeric example? Like with a coin or something?

Sure. Consider testing whether a coin is fair. Null: p = 0.5 (probability of heads). Suppose you flip 20 times and get 16 heads. A natural test statistic is “number of heads.” Under H0, X ~ Binomial(n=20, p=0.5). If you do a one-sided test for “coin is biased toward heads,” the p-value is: P(X ≥ 16 | n=20, p=0.5). Compute it: P(X ≥ 16) = sum_{k=16}^{20} C(20,k) * 0.5^20. Values: - C(20,16)=4845 - C(20,17)=1140 - C(20,18)=190 - C(20,19)=20 - C(20,20)=1 Sum = 4845+1140+190+20+1 = 6196 0.5^20 = 1/1,048,576 So p ≈ 6196 / 1,048,576 ≈ 0.00591. Meaning: if the coin were fair, getting 16 or more heads in 20 flips happens about 0.6% of the time. That’s a p-value: a probability of outcomes at least as extreme as observed under the null.
And if I did a two-sided test?

Two-sided for “coin is biased (either direction)” would count outcomes at least as extreme in either direction. “As extreme” typically means at least as far from expected (10 heads) as 16 is. 16 heads is +6 from expected. So the symmetric low tail would be ≤ 4 heads (10 − 6 = 4). Two-sided p-value = P(X ≥ 16 or X ≤ 4 | H0). We already have P(X ≥ 16) ≈ 0.00591. By symmetry, P(X ≤ 4) is the same as P(X ≥ 16) because p=0.5 and binomial is symmetric around 10: So two-sided p ≈ 2 * 0.00591 = 0.01182. (Depending on the exact two-sided definition for discrete distributions, there are slightly different conventions, but this is the common symmetric-tail version.)
So if I got 16 heads, the p-value says the chance of 16+ heads is 0.6% if fair. That makes sense. But why is it not the chance the coin is fair?

Because the p-value is computed assuming the coin is fair. It’s P(data | fair), not P(fair | data). To get P(fair | data), you’d need: - a prior probability that the coin is fair, - and a model for how it could be unfair (distribution over possible biases), then apply Bayes’ theorem to update. Without those, frequentist testing doesn’t produce a probability that the null is true.
But isn’t the null either true or false? Why talk about probabilities at all then?

In frequentist statistics, the probability is about the randomness in the data due to the sampling process, not about the hypothesis being random. The hypothesis is treated as a fixed statement about a parameter (e.g., p=0.5). The data are random because if you repeated sampling, you’d see different outcomes. So the p-value quantifies how surprising your observed data would be under a world where the null is true.
What about confidence intervals? People say “95% confidence interval” like it’s a 95% chance the true value is in it.

That’s another common misinterpretation. A 95% confidence interval procedure is constructed so that, in repeated sampling under the same conditions, 95% of the intervals you compute will contain the true parameter value. After you compute one interval from your data, the parameter is fixed; the interval is what varies across repetitions. So strictly speaking, the interval either contains the parameter or it doesn’t; there’s not a frequentist probability attached to that specific realized interval. However, many people use “contains” language informally. If you want a probability statement about the parameter given the observed interval, that’s more naturally Bayesian (credible intervals).
So p-values and confidence intervals are related though, right?

Yes. For many common tests, a two-sided hypothesis test at α = 0.05 corresponds to whether the 95% confidence interval includes the null value. Example: Testing H0: μ = 0 (two-sided). - If the 95% CI for μ excludes 0, then p < 0.05. - If it includes 0, then p ≥ 0.05. This equivalence holds for standard models (like normal with unknown variance using t) and for “matching” methods (same assumptions, same standard errors).
If I have a CI that barely excludes 0, is that basically p just below 0.05?

Exactly. “Barely excludes” corresponds to a test statistic just barely beyond the critical value, which corresponds to a p-value just below α. For a two-sided z-test at α=0.05, the critical value is about 1.96 in absolute value. If your z is 1.97, p is about 0.049. If your z is 1.95, p is about 0.051.
Why is 1.96 the cutoff?

For a standard normal Z ~ N(0,1), the two-sided 5% significance level corresponds to putting 2.5% in each tail. So you want z such that P(|Z| ≥ z) = 0.05. That means P(Z ≥ z) = 0.025. The 97.5th percentile of the standard normal is about 1.959964, commonly rounded to 1.96.
I hear people say “p-hacking” and “multiple comparisons.” How does that affect p-values?

The p-value calculation assumes a single pre-specified test. If you run many tests or try many analysis variants and then report only the smallest p-value, the chance of seeing a small p-value under the null can become much larger than 0.05. Multiple comparisons example: Suppose you test 20 independent null hypotheses, all true, each at α=0.05. - Expected number of false rejections is 20 * 0.05 = 1. - Probability of at least one false positive is 1 − (0.95)^20 ≈ 0.642. So you’re very likely to get something “significant” by chance if you do many tests. Corrections (Bonferroni, Holm, Benjamini-Hochberg, etc.) adjust thresholds or interpretive frameworks to account for this.
Bonferroni is divide by number of tests, right?

Yes, the simplest form: if you want to control family-wise error rate (FWER) at α overall across m tests, Bonferroni uses per-test threshold α/m. So if α=0.05 and m=20, you use 0.0025 for each test. It’s conservative, especially when tests are correlated, but it’s straightforward and guarantees FWER control under minimal assumptions.
What’s the difference between family-wise error and false discovery rate?

Family-wise error rate (FWER): probability of making at least one Type I error in the family of tests. - It’s strict: tries to avoid any false positives. False discovery rate (FDR): expected proportion of false positives among the rejected hypotheses. - It’s less strict and often more powerful when many tests are performed. Benjamini-Hochberg (BH) is a common procedure to control FDR.
Can you explain BH in a way I can follow?

Sure. Suppose you have m p-values from m tests: p1, p2, ..., pm. 1) Sort them ascending: p(1) ≤ p(2) ≤ ... ≤ p(m). 2) Choose desired FDR level q (e.g., 0.05). 3) Find the largest k such that p(k) ≤ (k/m) * q. 4) Reject all hypotheses corresponding to p(1) through p(k). Intuition: the threshold becomes less stringent as you move down the sorted list, allowing more rejections while controlling the expected false discovery proportion.
Is “statistically significant” the same as “important”?

No. Statistical significance is about whether the data are inconsistent with a null model given sample size and variability. Practical importance (or substantive importance) is about effect size, costs/benefits, real-world impact, etc. You can have: - statistically significant but tiny effect (large n), - non-significant but potentially large effect (small n, noisy data), - or anything in between. That’s why effect sizes and confidence intervals (or other uncertainty summaries) matter.
I’m also confused about “power.” People say low power means p-values are unreliable.

Power is the probability of rejecting the null when the alternative is true (i.e., detecting an effect of a specified size). It’s 1 − β, where β is Type II error rate. Low power has several consequences: - You’re less likely to detect real effects (more false negatives). - Among significant results, estimated effect sizes tend to be exaggerated on average (winner’s curse). - P-values can vary wildly between samples; replication is less consistent. But “unreliable” can mean different things. Low power doesn’t necessarily inflate the Type I error rate (if the test is conducted correctly), but it affects how informative significant/non-significant results are.
How do you compute power? Is it tied to p-values?

Power is computed from: - the assumed true effect size under the alternative, - sample size, - variability, - chosen significance level α, - and the test. For a simple z-test for a mean with known σ: - Test statistic: Z = (X̄ − μ0) / (σ/√n). - Under H0, Z ~ N(0,1). - Under an alternative μ = μ1, Z ~ N( (μ1 − μ0)/(σ/√n), 1 ). Power for a two-sided test is: P(|Z| ≥ z_{1−α/2} | μ=μ1). So yes, it’s connected: α sets the rejection region, and power is the probability the statistic lands there under the alternative distribution.
So if I lower α from 0.05 to 0.01, I reduce false positives but also reduce power?

Exactly, holding sample size constant. A smaller α makes the rejection region more extreme, so it’s harder to reject H0. That lowers Type I errors, but increases Type II errors (unless you increase sample size or the effect is large). There’s a tradeoff.
You mentioned effect size. Is there a standard definition?

“Effect size” depends on the setting, but common standardized ones include: - Cohen’s d for mean differences: d = (mean1 − mean2) / pooled SD. - Correlation r (already standardized). - Odds ratio / log odds ratio for binary outcomes. - Standardized regression coefficients (sometimes). Unstandardized effect sizes (raw differences) are often more interpretable: e.g., “difference in means is 2 units.” Standardized measures help compare across scales but can be abstract.
People also say “p-value is not the probability the result is due to chance.” Is that wrong too?

It’s misleading. Everything in the model includes randomness (“chance”), so saying “due to chance” is vague. A p-value is the probability of getting data at least as extreme as observed assuming the null model (often a “chance-only” model) is true. So in that sense it references chance. But it does not quantify the probability that chance alone produced the result, nor does it quantify the probability that the null is true. Better: “It quantifies how surprising the data are under the null model.”
What about the statement “p-value measures evidence against the null”?

It’s a common shorthand, and within a fixed framework it roughly aligns with “smaller p = more incompatibility with H0.” But as a strict measure of evidence, p-values have limitations: - They don’t incorporate a specific alternative. - They don’t account for prior plausibility. - They can be distorted by selection, optional stopping, multiple testing. Some evidence measures used instead/alongside include likelihood ratios, Bayes factors, information criteria, or estimation-focused approaches.
Bayes factor sounds like it actually compares hypotheses. How is it different from p-value?

A Bayes factor compares how well two models predict the observed data. For H0 and H1: BF10 = P(data | H1) / P(data | H0). - If BF10 = 10, the data are 10 times more likely under H1 than under H0. - If BF10 = 0.1, the data are 10 times more likely under H0. This is different from p-values because: - It’s a ratio of predictive probabilities, not a tail-area under one model. - It requires specifying H1 (including priors over parameters under H1).
But then you have to pick priors, which feels subjective.

It can be, but there are approaches: - “Objective” or weakly informative priors, - sensitivity analysis (show results across plausible priors), - default Bayes factors for standard tests. Also, frequentist methods have choices too (α threshold, test statistic, stopping rules, model assumptions). So “subjective” vs “objective” is more about being explicit about choices.
Another thing: if p = 0.2, does that mean there’s no effect?

No. A large p-value means the data are not very incompatible with the null; it does not prove the null is true. Possible reasons for p=0.2: - The true effect is zero. - The true effect is nonzero but small. - The sample is too small/noisy to detect it (low power). - Model assumptions are off. To argue for “no effect” in a meaningful way, you’d typically look at confidence intervals (are effects of practical size excluded?) or do equivalence testing.
Equivalence testing? That’s new to me.

Equivalence testing flips the question. Instead of trying to reject “effect = 0,” you try to reject “effect is meaningfully large.” You define a smallest effect size of interest (SESOI), say between −δ and +δ is “practically negligible.” Null for equivalence test: effect ≤ −δ or effect ≥ +δ (i.e., outside the equivalence bounds). Alternative: effect is between −δ and +δ. A common method is TOST (two one-sided tests): - Test 1: effect > −δ - Test 2: effect < +δ If both are significant at α, you conclude equivalence (effect is within bounds).
So you can get “significant” in equivalence testing meaning you found no meaningful effect?
Yes, “significant equivalence” means you have enough evidence that the effect is small enough (within your bounds). It’s a different question than “is it nonzero?”
Coming back to p-values: why do we condition on the null? Why not compute probability under the alternative?

Because in classic hypothesis testing, the null is used to derive a reference distribution for the test statistic—something you can compute without specifying a detailed alternative. You could compute how likely the data are under a specific alternative, but there are infinitely many alternatives. Frequentist testing often focuses on controlling error rates under H0, and uses power analyses to study behavior under alternatives. If you do specify a particular alternative (or a family of them), then likelihood ratio tests and Bayesian methods naturally incorporate P(data | alternative).
Are p-values uniform under the null? I heard that somewhere.

Yes (approximately, and exactly under some conditions). If the null hypothesis is true and the p-value is computed correctly from a continuous test statistic, then the p-value is uniformly distributed on [0,1]. Meaning: P(p ≤ x | H0) = x for 0 ≤ x ≤ 1. This property is why α-level tests work: under H0, the chance p ≤ α is α. For discrete tests (like the binomial example), p-values are not exactly uniform; they’re stochastically larger than uniform (conservative) unless you randomize.
If p-values are uniform under the null, does that mean a p=0.01 happens 1% of the time even when the null is true?

More precisely: under H0, the probability that the p-value is ≤ 0.01 is 1%. So across many repeated tests where H0 is true, about 1% will yield p ≤ 0.01. But seeing p=0.01 in a single experiment doesn’t tell you the probability H0 is true; it tells you that your observed outcome is among the more extreme 1% of outcomes expected under H0.
How does sample size affect p-values? I feel like bigger sample = smaller p.

Often, yes, because many test statistics scale with √n. For example, in a z-test for a mean difference: Z = (estimate − null value) / SE, and SE often shrinks like 1/√n. So if the estimate stays similar but n increases, SE decreases, Z grows, and p decreases. This is why very small effects can become statistically significant with large samples. The p-value reflects a combination of effect size and precision.
So should we stop using p-values?

Not necessarily. They’re useful if: - the test is pre-specified, - assumptions are reasonable, - multiple testing is handled, - and results are interpreted alongside effect sizes and uncertainty. Problems often come from misuse: treating p<0.05 as a stamp of truth, ignoring model assumptions, selective reporting, and binary thinking.
Binary thinking meaning “significant” vs “not significant”?

Exactly. The practice of turning a continuous measure (p) into a hard yes/no at 0.05 can be misleading. Evidence and uncertainty are gradual, and the cutoff can encourage overinterpretation of tiny differences around 0.05. Many recommend reporting: - the exact p-value, - effect size estimate, - confidence interval, - and possibly pre-registered analysis plans or adjusted p-values when relevant.
If p=0.049 and p=0.051 are basically the same, why do people treat them differently?

Because decision rules require thresholds if you must make a decision. But scientifically, they’re extremely similar evidence against H0. The “cliff” at 0.05 is a human convention. The underlying data differences are usually tiny, and the uncertainty is large. Treating 0.049 as “real” and 0.051 as “no effect” is an exaggeration.
Can you relate p-values to z-scores more? I think in z-scores.

Sure. For a two-sided z-test: p = 2 * (1 − Φ(|z|)), where Φ is the standard normal CDF. Some common mappings: - |z| = 1.645 → p ≈ 0.10 (two-sided) - |z| = 1.96 → p ≈ 0.05 - |z| = 2.576 → p ≈ 0.01 - |z| = 3.291 → p ≈ 0.001 So if you know z, you can approximate p quickly.
And for t-scores?

Similar, but you use the t distribution with df degrees of freedom: p = 2 * (1 − F_t(df)(|t|)). As df increases, the t distribution approaches the normal. For small df, tails are heavier, so for the same |t| you get a larger p than with z.
Why is t heavier-tailed?

Because when you estimate the standard deviation from the sample, that introduces extra uncertainty. The t distribution accounts for that by having heavier tails, especially with small sample sizes. As sample size grows, the SD estimate stabilizes, and t approaches normal.
Another phrase I hate is “reject the null.” That sounds like you proved it false.

Right—“reject” is a decision under a rule, not a proof. It means “the data fall in a region that would be rare under H0.” In frequentist testing, you control error rates, but you never get certainty. A better mental model: you’re choosing between “retain H0 for now” and “treat H0 as implausible enough to act as if it’s false,” given the pre-set tolerance for false positives.
If I fail to reject, does that mean I accept the null?

No. “Fail to reject” just means you didn’t see strong enough evidence against H0 given your threshold and test. It could be because: - H0 is true, or - the test lacked power, or - data are too noisy, etc. Some people say “do not reject” rather than “accept.”
What about “marginally significant” like p=0.06?

It’s a fuzzy term. It often means “close to 0.05 but not below.” The issue is it can encourage interpreting p=0.06 as “almost true” while p=0.04 is “true,” which is not a reliable distinction. If you report p=0.06, best practice is to report it plainly and interpret cautiously, focusing on effect estimates and uncertainty rather than inventing a special category.
Is there any way to interpret p-values as evidence if we assume some prior?

Yes. If you adopt a Bayesian model, you can compute posterior probabilities or Bayes factors. You can also connect p-values to bounds on Bayes factors under certain assumptions (like the Sellke–Bayarri–Berger bound) showing that p-values often overstate evidence against H0. For example, one bound says the minimum possible Bayes factor in favor of H0 given a p-value p (for p ≤ 1/e) is roughly: BF01 ≥ −e * p * ln(p). This implies that even p=0.05 may correspond to only modest evidence against H0 under reasonable models.
Can you compute that for p=0.05?

Using the bound: BF01 ≥ −e * p * ln(p). Take p=0.05. ln(0.05) ≈ −2.9957. So −e * 0.05 * (−2.9957) = e * 0.05 * 2.9957. e ≈ 2.7183. 2.7183 * 0.05 ≈ 0.1359. 0.1359 * 2.9957 ≈ 0.407. So BF01 ≥ 0.407, meaning the evidence against H0 (BF10) is at most about 1/0.407 ≈ 2.46 to 1, under that bound. That’s not huge. (This is a bound, not an exact conversion; actual Bayes factors depend on the alternative model.)
So p=0.05 could correspond to only like 2.5:1 odds? That’s way less than I assumed.
Yes, that’s one reason people caution against treating p=0.05 as “strong evidence.” It can be compatible with relatively weak evidence depending on the alternative.
I also get confused about “significance level” vs “p-value.” They sound too similar.

Common confusion. Distinguish: - Significance level α: a threshold you choose before seeing data, controlling Type I error rate of the procedure. - p-value: a number computed from the data, summarizing extremeness under H0. Decision: compare p to α. - If p ≤ α: reject H0. - If p > α: fail to reject H0.
Does α have to be 0.05? Could I choose 0.2?

You can choose any α, but it changes your false positive tolerance. α=0.2 means that if H0 is true, you will reject it 20% of the time in repeated use. That’s a high false positive rate. Sometimes exploratory analyses use more lenient thresholds, but then claims should be correspondingly cautious, and ideally validated with new data or stronger confirmatory analyses.
What about p=0.001? Is that “definitely true”?

It’s still not “definitely.” It indicates that your observed result would be very rare under H0 (0.1% or less), assuming assumptions and no selection effects. That’s strong incompatibility with H0. But: - model violations can produce small p-values, - multiple testing can produce small p-values by chance, - and it still doesn’t directly give P(H1|data). It’s stronger evidence against H0 than p=0.05 in the “surprising under H0” sense, but not absolute proof.
Can assumptions really matter that much?

Yes. P-values are only valid under the test’s assumptions (distributional assumptions, independence, correct model specification, etc.). If assumptions are violated, the null distribution of the test statistic may not be what you think, so the p-value can be inaccurate (too small or too large). Robust methods, permutation tests, bootstrap, or model checks can help.
Permutation tests—do those give p-values too?

Yes. A permutation test constructs a reference distribution by rearranging labels in the data in a way that’s valid under H0. Procedure: 1) Compute a test statistic on the observed data. 2) Randomly permute labels many times; compute the statistic each time. 3) The p-value is the fraction of permuted statistics at least as extreme as the observed statistic. This gives a p-value that’s valid under the null of exchangeability (a kind of “no effect” assumption) without relying on parametric distributions.
Why do permutation tests sometimes give slightly different p-values each run?

If you use a finite number of random permutations (Monte Carlo approximation), there’s simulation error. More permutations reduces the variability. Also, there are conventions about adding 1 to numerator/denominator to avoid zero p-values: p ≈ (b + 1) / (B + 1), where b is number of permuted stats at least as extreme, and B is number of permutations.
I’m trying to reconcile this: p-value is tail probability under H0. But then what is the “likelihood”?

Likelihood is proportional to P(data | parameter), treated as a function of the parameter given fixed observed data. Example: coin flips with k heads out of n. Likelihood L(p) ∝ p^k (1−p)^(n−k). The p-value is not the likelihood. It integrates tail areas of a test statistic distribution under a specific parameter value (the null). Likelihood evaluates how plausible different parameter values are in explaining the observed data, without tail areas.
So likelihood seems closer to what I want: which hypothesis is more supported.

Likelihood can compare point hypotheses via likelihood ratios, and it forms the basis for many inference methods (MLE, likelihood ratio tests, Bayesian updating). But to compare a point null vs a composite alternative, you still need to specify the alternative (range of parameter values) and possibly integrate (Bayes) or maximize (frequentist LRT).
Is a likelihood ratio test p-value still a p-value?

Yes. In a likelihood ratio test (LRT), you compute a statistic like: Λ = 2( log L(θ̂_full) − log L(θ̂_null) ). Under certain regularity conditions and when H0 is true, Λ approximately follows a chi-square distribution with degrees of freedom equal to the difference in number of parameters. Then the p-value is: p = P(χ²_df ≥ Λ | H0). So it’s still a tail-area probability under H0.
Chi-square p-values confuse me too. Same idea though?

Same idea. You compute a chi-square statistic measuring discrepancy between observed and expected counts (or other model-based quantities). Under H0, that statistic has (approximately) a chi-square distribution, and the p-value is the probability of getting a chi-square statistic at least as large as observed. Because chi-square is nonnegative and larger values mean more discrepancy, it’s typically a one-sided tail: P(χ² ≥ observed).
So when people say “p-value is the probability the null is true,” it’s just flat wrong.

Correct. It’s a different conditional probability. If you want “probability the null is true,” you need a Bayesian posterior probability, which depends on prior odds and the marginal likelihoods under each hypothesis.
Could you show Bayes theorem in this setting, just to see the structure?

Sure. Consider two hypotheses H0 and H1. Bayes theorem gives: Posterior odds = Prior odds × Bayes factor P(H1 | data) / P(H0 | data) = [P(H1) / P(H0)] × [P(data | H1) / P(data | H0)]. The second ratio is the Bayes factor BF10. If you know prior odds and can compute BF10, you can get posterior odds, then posterior probabilities. None of these are provided by a p-value alone.
If that’s the case, why do p-values feel like they’re everywhere?

They’re historically entrenched, relatively easy to compute, and they give a standardized decision rule with error-rate guarantees under repeated sampling. They’re also widely taught and supported in software. The downside is they’re easy to misinterpret and can encourage simplistic conclusions if used without context like effect sizes, uncertainty, and design considerations.
Is there a way to interpret p-values correctly but still make decisions?

Yes: 1) Pre-specify α and analysis plan. 2) Use p-values as one part of evidence, not the whole story. 3) Report effect sizes and intervals. 4) Adjust for multiple comparisons if applicable. 5) Consider power and design. 6) Be transparent about all tests run. Then a decision can be framed as: “Given our error tolerance (α) and assumptions, this result crosses the threshold for rejecting H0.”
I’m still hung up on the “assuming the null is true” part. If I already think the null is unlikely, then p-values seem pointless.

If you strongly believe the null is unlikely, then a test of a point null might not be the most informative question. You might focus on estimating the effect size precisely (estimation) rather than testing whether it’s exactly zero. However, p-values can still be useful for: - checking whether data are compatible with a specific null model, - comparing nested models, - or providing a standardized signal when you need a formal decision rule. But if the null is a strawman, emphasis on estimation and model comparison might be better.
Point null meaning exactly zero effect?

Yes. Many null hypotheses are “exactly zero difference” or “exactly no association.” In reality, exact zeros may be rare; effects might be tiny but nonzero. Testing a point null can become a test of whether your sample size is large enough to detect tiny deviations. That’s why equivalence testing or estimation with practical thresholds can be more aligned with real questions, depending on goals.
But you said no mention of context—so let’s keep it abstract. In abstract terms, how do I decide what null to test?

Abstractly: - A null hypothesis should represent a meaningful reference model: a baseline, a constraint, or a simplified model you’d like to challenge. - It should correspond to a distribution for your test statistic that you can justify. - If “exactly zero” is not meaningful, use a null that reflects “no practically meaningful effect,” which leads to interval nulls or equivalence frameworks. So: choose H0 based on what constitutes “no meaningful deviation” and what assumptions you can defend.
Interval nulls? Like H0: effect is between -0.1 and 0.1?

Exactly. That’s an interval null (or “region of practical equivalence” in Bayesian language). Instead of a single value, H0 covers a range of small effects. Frequentist approaches include equivalence tests; Bayesian approaches compute posterior probability that the parameter lies within that interval.
If I do a regular t-test, it’s a point null. Is that why p-values can be tiny even for tiny effects?

Yes. With large n, you can detect very small departures from the point null. Because the standard error shrinks, the test statistic grows even for small effects, making p-values small. This is a feature of the mathematics: as n → ∞, almost any nonzero effect will become statistically significant.
Then shouldn’t we always report effect size with p-value?

That’s a strong recommendation. P-values alone don’t tell magnitude. Reporting: - estimate, - standard error, - confidence interval, - and p-value gives a fuller picture: size, uncertainty, and compatibility with a null.
Sometimes I see “highly significant” for p<0.001. Is that okay?

It’s common, but it can reinforce binary thinking. If used, it should still be paired with effect size and uncertainty. “Highly significant” refers only to the p-value being very small under H0, not to the effect being large or important.
What about the phrase “trend toward significance”?
Similar issue. It often means p is between 0.05 and 0.10, but it can be a way of implying evidence without meeting a threshold. Better to just report the p-value and interval and let readers judge.
Are p-values comparable across different tests?

Not always in a straightforward way. A p-value is calibrated under its own null distribution and test statistic. A p=0.01 from one test and p=0.01 from another both mean “1% tail area under the respective null,” but the tests could have different sensitivities, assumptions, and what “extreme” means. As a rough measure of incompatibility with each null, they’re comparable in the sense of tail probability. But comparing p-values across different models and data structures can be misleading.
If p-values are tail probabilities, why are they sometimes computed from approximations?

Because exact null distributions can be complicated or unknown. Many tests rely on asymptotic results (large-sample approximations), like: - z approximations, - chi-square approximations (LRT), - normal approximations for many estimators. When sample sizes are small or conditions aren’t met, approximations may be poor. Then exact tests (Fisher’s exact), permutation tests, or simulation-based methods can give more accurate p-values.
Fisher’s exact test gives exact p-values?

For the null of independence in a 2×2 contingency table with fixed margins, yes—it computes the exact hypergeometric tail probability of tables as extreme or more extreme than observed. Even there, “as extreme” can be defined in different ways (one-sided vs two-sided, and choice of extremeness measure), but the distribution under the null is exact given the conditioning.
So even “two-sided p-value” can be ambiguous?

In discrete settings, yes. For continuous symmetric distributions, two-sided is typically unambiguous: double the smaller tail probability or use |T|. For discrete distributions, you can’t always get exact symmetry, and “double the one-sided p” can exceed 1 or not correspond to a set of outcomes with probability exactly 2*tail. Alternatives include: - doubling the smaller tail and capping at 1, - using “probability of outcomes with probability ≤ that of observed” (Fisher’s two-sided), - mid-p adjustments, etc. So you should know which convention is used.
Mid-p?

Mid-p is a compromise for discrete tests that reduces conservativeness. Instead of counting the entire probability of the observed table in the tail, you count half of it: p_mid = P(more extreme) + 0.5 * P(observed). It can improve calibration in some senses but loses the strict “p-values are stochastically ≥ uniform” property.
This is a lot. Can we summarize the correct definition one more time?

Definition: Given a null hypothesis H0 and a test statistic T with known (or approximated) distribution under H0, after observing T_obs, the p-value is: p = P(T is at least as extreme as T_obs | H0). “Extreme” is defined by the alternative (one-sided) or by distance from null in both directions (two-sided).
And the main wrong interpretation is flipping it to P(H0 | data).
Exactly.
Another confusion: if p=0.03, does that mean if I repeated the experiment I’d get significance 97% of the time?

No. That would be power (probability of significance under a true alternative), not 1 − p. Replication probability depends on the true effect size and variability. A single p=0.03 result could replicate with much lower or higher probability depending on those factors. P-values are noisy; they vary a lot between samples.
Is there a way to see that variability?

Yes. Under a fixed true effect, the test statistic has a distribution across repeated samples, so p-values also have a distribution (not uniform unless the null is true). You can simulate: - pick a true effect size, - simulate many datasets, - compute p-values, - look at their histogram. You’d see a pile-up near 0 when the effect is large and power is high, and a flatter distribution when effect is small.
Suppose the null is true. Then p-values are uniform. That means sometimes you’ll get p=0.0001 even though null is true?

Yes, rarely. Under uniform(0,1), the probability p ≤ 0.0001 is 0.0001 (0.01%). So in 10,000 true-null tests, you expect about one p-value that small by chance. That’s why with many tests, very small p-values can occur even if all nulls are true—multiple testing again.
People also say “statistical significance is not scientific significance.” That seems related.

Right. Statistical significance is about a probabilistic threshold under a model. Scientific (or practical) significance is about magnitude, relevance, and consequences. The p-value doesn’t encode those.
Is there any reason to prefer p-values over confidence intervals?

They answer slightly different questions: - p-value: compatibility of data with a specific null. - confidence interval: range of parameter values compatible with data (in the sense of the CI procedure). Intervals are often more informative because they show both direction and magnitude with uncertainty. But if you need a formal test decision, p-values (or equivalent CI exclusion) provide that.
If I have a 95% CI, can I get the p-value from it?

Often you can approximate: - If the null value is outside the 95% CI, p < 0.05 (two-sided). - If inside, p > 0.05. To get the exact p-value, you need the test statistic or standard error. In many symmetric cases: estimate ± 1.96*SE is the 95% CI, so SE ≈ (upper − lower)/(2*1.96), then z = estimate/SE (if null is 0), then p from z.
That’s helpful. So p-values are basically about standardized distance from null?
In many common tests, yes: p-values are monotone functions of a standardized statistic (estimate − null)/SE. But not always (e.g., some nonparametric tests or complex models).
What about nonparametric tests like Mann-Whitney? Is p-value same idea?

Same idea: define a test statistic (based on ranks), derive its distribution under H0 (exact or approximate), then compute tail probability. Interpretation is still “probability of observing a statistic this extreme under H0,” where H0 is often “the two distributions are identical” (or sometimes “probability of one exceeding the other is 0.5,” depending on assumptions).
I’ve heard that Mann-Whitney tests medians. Is that true?

Not exactly. Mann–Whitney U tests whether one distribution tends to produce larger values than the other. Under certain conditions (same shape distributions differing only by location shift), it can be interpreted as a test of median difference. But in general, it’s about stochastic dominance or the probability that a random draw from one group exceeds the other. So p-value still works the same way, but the null and effect interpretation differ.
Another thing: Why do we use “p” for p-value? Is it just “probability”?
Historically, yes: “p” for probability. But the p-value is a particular probability, not any probability. Its meaning depends on the test.
If I want to avoid misinterpretation, what exact sentence should I use when reporting?

A safe template (abstract): “Using [test], we tested H0: [statement]. The observed test statistic was [value], corresponding to p = [value], meaning that under H0, the probability of obtaining a test statistic at least as extreme as observed is [p].” If you want shorter: “Under H0, results as extreme as observed would occur with probability p = ...”
Is it okay to say “unlikely under the null”?
Yes, that’s a good shorthand. Just keep “under the null” explicit.
Can p-values ever be exactly zero?

In theory, with continuous distributions and finite samples, p-values are > 0. But software sometimes prints 0 due to rounding (e.g., p < 2e−16). In permutation tests with finite permutations, you can get 0 if you don’t use the +1 correction; that’s why people use (b+1)/(B+1).
What does “exact p-value” mean then?

It means computed from the exact finite-sample null distribution rather than an asymptotic approximation. For example: - exact binomial test, - Fisher’s exact test, - exact permutation distribution (if enumerated). “Exact” doesn’t mean “more true,” just that it matches the exact probability model under H0 for that finite sample.
I also see “one-tailed p-value is half the two-tailed” sometimes. Is that always?

Only when: - the null distribution is symmetric, - the observed statistic is on the side you’re testing, - and the two-sided p-value is computed as 2*one-sided tail. In general, not always—especially for asymmetric distributions or discrete tests with different two-sided conventions.
Can you show an example where it’s not half?

Discrete case: Suppose a test statistic has a discrete distribution where tail probabilities are not symmetric around the observed statistic. A two-sided p-value might be defined as “sum of probabilities of outcomes with probability ≤ P(observed).” That won’t necessarily equal 2 times the one-sided tail. Also, if the observed statistic is negative but you compute a one-sided p for positive direction, the one-sided p will be large, not half.
Got it. Another term: “alpha spending” and “optional stopping.” How does stopping affect p-values?

Classic p-values assume a fixed sampling plan. If you repeatedly check p-values as data accumulate and stop when p < 0.05, the actual Type I error rate becomes larger than 0.05. This is optional stopping (data-dependent stopping). To maintain error control, you need methods like: - sequential analysis (e.g., group sequential designs), - alpha spending functions, - or pre-specified stopping rules with adjusted thresholds.
So if someone collects data until significant, the p-value is biased downward?

Yes, it tends to produce smaller p-values more often under the null than it should, because you’re effectively doing multiple looks at the data. The reported p-value no longer has its nominal interpretation.
Is there any p-value method that’s valid under optional stopping?

In frequentist terms, you can design sequential tests that maintain Type I error control with proper critical boundaries. In Bayesian terms, Bayes factors under certain conditions can be less sensitive to optional stopping (though priors and model assumptions matter). There are also “always-valid p-values” and e-values in modern sequential inference frameworks, but those are more specialized.
E-values?

An e-value is a nonnegative statistic with expectation ≤ 1 under H0. You can use it for sequential testing because Ville’s inequality gives control over stopping times. It’s an alternative framework to p-values that can be robust to optional stopping. But it’s less commonly taught.
This is getting advanced. Back to basics: What is the relationship between p-value and “significance”?

“Significance” is a label based on comparing p to α. If p ≤ α, the result is “statistically significant at level α.” So significance depends on both the p-value and the chosen α. The p-value itself is just a number.
Is there any reason to report p-values at all if we have intervals?

Some reasons: - In some fields, decision rules are entrenched. - For model comparison or nested hypotheses, p-values provide a conventional test. - For quick screening, p-values can be a compact summary. But many recommend focusing on estimation and intervals. Still, p-values can be included as long as they’re interpreted correctly.
What about “null hypothesis significance testing” (NHST)? Is that the whole p<0.05 thing?

NHST is the framework combining: - a null hypothesis, - a test statistic, - a p-value, - and a decision rule at α. It’s often associated with the “p<0.05 therefore effect is real” culture. Critiques of NHST focus on its misuse, dichotomization, and misinterpretation.
Are there alternatives to NHST that are still frequentist?

Yes: - Estimation with confidence intervals and emphasis on effect sizes. - Model selection via AIC/BIC (not purely frequentist in interpretation but widely used). - Likelihood-based inference (likelihood ratios). - Prediction-focused evaluation (cross-validation, predictive performance). - Equivalence and non-inferiority testing (still hypothesis tests, but different nulls).
AIC/BIC aren’t p-values. What do they represent?

They’re criteria balancing fit and complexity. AIC = 2k − 2 log L, where k is number of parameters, L is maximized likelihood. Lower AIC indicates better estimated out-of-sample predictive performance (under certain approximations). BIC = k log n − 2 log L, which penalizes complexity more strongly as n grows; it’s related to approximating Bayes factors under certain priors. They’re for comparing models, not for testing a single null at a fixed α.
In basic terms, p-values are about surprise. AIC is about prediction. Bayes factors are about relative support.
That’s a good high-level summary.
Can we go back to that coin example and do a confidence interval there?

Sure. Coin flips: n=20, k=16, observed proportion p̂ = 0.8. A simple approximate 95% CI for the true p is: p̂ ± 1.96 * sqrt(p̂(1−p̂)/n). Compute SE ≈ sqrt(0.8*0.2/20) = sqrt(0.16/20) = sqrt(0.008) ≈ 0.08944. Margin ≈ 1.96*0.08944 ≈ 0.1753. So CI ≈ 0.8 ± 0.175 → (0.625, 0.975). This is the Wald interval; for small n it can be rough. Better is Wilson interval or exact (Clopper–Pearson). But note: 0.5 is not in (0.625, 0.975), consistent with the small p-value we got.
So if 0.5 is outside the 95% CI, that corresponds to p<0.05.
Exactly (for the matching two-sided test). Here, 0.5 is well outside, so p is well below 0.05.
What if I flip 20 times and get 12 heads. p-value?

Under H0: p=0.5, X~Binomial(20,0.5). Observed X=12. Two-sided p-value (symmetric tails): distance from 10 is 2, so include X ≥ 12 and X ≤ 8. Compute P(X ≥ 12) and double it (by symmetry): P(X ≥ 12) = sum_{k=12}^{20} C(20,k) / 2^20. We can compute: C(20,12)=125970 C(20,13)=77520 C(20,14)=38760 C(20,15)=15504 C(20,16)=4845 C(20,17)=1140 C(20,18)=190 C(20,19)=20 C(20,20)=1 Sum = 125970+77520+38760+15504+4845+1140+190+20+1 = 263,950. Divide by 2^20 = 1,048,576: P(X ≥ 12) ≈ 0.2517. Two-sided p ≈ 2*0.2517 = 0.5034. So p about 0.50: extremely compatible with fairness.
That makes sense. p-values near 0.5 mean “very typical under null.”

Yes. Under H0, most p-values fall around the middle because uniform distribution has equal density everywhere, but “typical” test statistics correspond to moderate p-values. Very small p-values correspond to extreme statistics.
Wait, uniform means small p-values are just as dense as big p-values.

Correct in density. But “typical p-value” under uniform is not necessarily 0.5; any interval of equal length has equal probability. Still, the median is 0.5, and extreme small p-values are rare because they occupy small intervals. For example, P(p ≤ 0.05)=0.05, P(p ≤ 0.5)=0.5.
Another question: If I run the same test twice and get p=0.04 then p=0.2, what does that mean?

It means p-values vary across samples. Even if there is a true effect (or even if there isn’t), sampling variability can produce different p-values. Under a true null, p-values are uniform, so such variation is expected. Under a true alternative, p-values tend to be smaller on average, but still variable—especially if power is moderate. So you can’t treat a single p-value as a stable “property” of the phenomenon; it’s a random variable.
Is there a way to combine p-values across repetitions?

Yes, meta-analysis techniques. For combining p-values specifically: - Fisher’s method: combine via −2 Σ log(pi), which follows χ² with 2k df under H0. - Stouffer’s method: combine z-scores, possibly weighted. But often better is to combine effect estimates with standard errors (fixed or random effects meta-analysis), which gives a pooled estimate and interval.
Fisher’s method: can you show the formula?
If you have k independent p-values p1...pk under the global null, define: X = −2 ∑_{i=1}^k ln(pi). Under H0, X ~ χ² with 2k degrees of freedom. Then you compute a combined p-value as P(χ²_{2k} ≥ X).
Independence matters?
Yes. If p-values are dependent, Fisher’s null distribution changes. There are extensions and approximations for dependence, but independence (or certain dependence structures) is typically assumed.
People also talk about “effect of p-values being sensitive to model misspecification.” Can you give an example?

One example: Suppose you use a t-test assuming independent observations, but the data are correlated (clustered). The standard error estimate is too small, making the t-statistic too large, yielding artificially small p-values (inflated Type I error). Correcting for clustering (cluster-robust SE) changes the null distribution and p-values. Another: non-normal heavy tails with small samples can make t-test p-values inaccurate (though t-test is fairly robust to moderate non-normality).
So p-values are only as good as the standard errors.
In many tests, yes. Since test statistics often are estimate/SE, if SE is wrong, p-values can be wrong.
Is that why robust standard errors exist?
Exactly. Robust (sandwich) standard errors aim to remain valid under heteroskedasticity or certain misspecifications, improving inference.
But robust SEs can change p-values a lot, right?
They can. If the original model underestimated variability, robust SEs increase SE, reducing the test statistic magnitude, increasing p-values. Or sometimes the opposite.
I feel like p-values are a minefield. Is there any “safe” way to think about them?

A safe mental model: - A p-value is a diagnostic of compatibility between data and a specific null model. - Small p: data look unusual if the null model were true. - Large p: data look typical under the null model. - It is not a probability that the null is true, not a measure of effect size, and not a guarantee of replication. And always remember: it is conditional on the model and on a pre-specified analysis plan.
If I want to interpret p-values as evidence, should I switch to likelihood ratios?

Likelihood ratios can be more directly “evidence-like” because they compare how well two hypotheses predict the observed data. But you must specify the alternative. If you can do that, likelihood ratios or Bayes factors provide a more direct comparison than p-values. Still, p-values can be fine if your goal is error-rate-controlled testing rather than evidential comparison.
Can you show a likelihood ratio for the coin example?

Sure. Observed k=16 heads out of n=20. Likelihood under H0: p=0.5: L0 = (0.5)^20 (ignoring the combinatorial constant, but it cancels in ratios if both models include it). For a simple alternative H1: p=0.8 (just as an example point alternative): L1 = (0.8)^16 * (0.2)^4. Likelihood ratio: LR = L1/L0 = [(0.8)^16 (0.2)^4] / (0.5)^20. Compute logs to see magnitude: log LR = 16 log(0.8) + 4 log(0.2) − 20 log(0.5). log(0.8)≈−0.22314, so 16*−0.22314≈−3.570. log(0.2)≈−1.60944, so 4*−1.60944≈−6.438. −20 log(0.5)= −20*(−0.6931)= +13.862. Sum ≈ −3.570 −6.438 +13.862 = 3.854. So LR ≈ e^{3.854} ≈ 47.2. So the observed data are about 47 times more likely under p=0.8 than under p=0.5 (for that chosen point alternative).
That seems more intuitive than p-values.

Many people find it more intuitive because it’s directly comparing predictions. But note: choosing p=0.8 as the alternative was arbitrary. If H1 allowed many p values, you’d need to integrate or maximize, leading to different comparisons.
If I maximize likelihood over p, that’s p-hat = 0.8. Then LR compares p=0.8 vs p=0.5?

Yes, that’s the generalized likelihood ratio (GLR) for testing p=0.5: LR = L(p̂)/L(0.5), where p̂ = k/n. That’s related to likelihood ratio tests, which then use asymptotic chi-square distributions to derive p-values.
So p-values come back again.
Right. Many routes lead back to p-values if you want frequentist calibration and decisions. But you can still use likelihood ratios as descriptive evidence without converting to p-values.
Last question (for now): If someone says “p=0.03 means there is a 3% chance the result is a fluke,” what’s the correction?

The correction is: “p=0.03 means that if the null model were true, the probability of obtaining a result at least as extreme as observed is 3%.” Calling it “a 3% chance it’s a fluke” is ambiguous and often implies “3% chance the null is true,” which is wrong. Also “fluke” suggests a causal story; p-values don’t provide that.
Okay. I think I finally get it: p-values are about how surprising the data are assuming the null, not about the probability the hypothesis is true.
Exactly. That’s the core. If you keep the conditioning “under the null” in your head, most misinterpretations become easier to avoid.
Actually, can we keep going a bit? I want to understand “null model” vs “null hypothesis.” Are those different?

They’re related but not identical. - Null hypothesis: typically a statement about a parameter or a relationship (e.g., θ = 0). - Null model: the full probabilistic model implied by the null hypothesis plus additional assumptions (distributional form, independence, variance structure, etc.). You need the null model to compute the distribution of the test statistic and thus the p-value. Often, people say “null hypothesis” but they’re implicitly assuming a whole model.
So if the model assumptions are wrong, the p-value is wrong even if the null statement θ=0 is the thing being tested.

Correct. For example, θ=0 might be the null statement, but if you assume normal errors and they’re not, or assume independence and data are correlated, the reference distribution under the null may be wrong, so the p-value can be miscalibrated.
Is that why diagnostics matter?
Yes. Model checking, diagnostics, residual analysis, sensitivity analyses, and robust methods are important to ensure that inference (including p-values) is meaningful.
I think I’ve asked enough. But can you give me a final compact “what p-value is” and “what it isn’t” list?

What it is: - A tail-area probability of getting data (via a test statistic) at least as extreme as observed, assuming the null model is true. What it isn’t: - Not P(H0 is true | data). - Not the probability the result is “due to chance” in a causal sense. - Not a measure of effect size or importance. - Not the probability of replication. - Not immune to multiple testing, optional stopping, or model misspecification.
Thanks. That list is going in my notes.
I can compute eigenvalues fine but I have no intuition for what they “mean” geometrically. Can you explain?

Think of a linear map A as something that takes the whole space and “pushes” it around: it can stretch, shrink, shear, rotate, reflect, and collapse dimensions. Most directions in space get sent to some other direction. An eigenvector is a special direction that does not change direction under the map—only its length (and possibly its sign/phase) changes. The eigenvalue is the factor by which that length changes. Geometrically in 2D: imagine drawing many arrows from the origin in all directions. Apply A to each arrow. Usually the arrow points somewhere else. But if there’s an eigenvector v, then Av lies on the same line as v. So that line through the origin is invariant: the map sends the line to itself. The eigenvalue λ tells you the scale along that line: Av = λv. If λ > 1, vectors on that line stretch away from the origin; if 0 < λ < 1, they contract toward the origin; if λ < 0, they flip across the origin and scale by |λ|; if λ = 0, the whole line collapses to the origin. In higher dimensions, it’s the same: eigenvectors pick out invariant 1D subspaces (lines). More generally, when you have complex eigenvalues, the invariant “thing” is often a 2D plane where the map acts like a rotation+scaling.
I get that “direction preserved” idea. But why should those directions exist at all? For a random transformation, why would any direction map to itself?

In 2D over the reals, they don’t always exist: a pure rotation by 90° has no real eigenvectors because every direction rotates to a different direction. That’s a good sanity check: eigenvectors are not guaranteed over the reals. Over the complex numbers, eigenvalues always exist for any square matrix (by the fundamental theorem of algebra applied to the characteristic polynomial). Geometrically, allowing complex scalars means you’re allowing “directions” in a complexified sense. But there’s also a purely real geometric story: if a real 2×2 matrix has complex conjugate eigenvalues a ± bi, then it has an invariant plane (the whole 2D space in that case), and the action is equivalent to a rotation by angle θ with a uniform scaling factor r, where r = √(a² + b²) and θ = atan2(b, a). So even when you don’t have a real invariant line, you still have an invariant plane with a consistent rotation+scaling behavior. In 3D and higher, invariant lines are more common, but still not guaranteed over the reals for arbitrary matrices. However, many matrices arising from “stretching/shearing” do have real eigenvectors.
Can you show me a very visual example where eigenvectors are obvious without computing?

Sure. Consider a simple scaling map in 2D: A = [[2, 0], [0, 1/2]]. This stretches x-coordinates by 2 and shrinks y-coordinates by 1/2. The x-axis is clearly invariant: any vector on the x-axis stays on the x-axis, scaled by 2. So any nonzero vector along (1,0) is an eigenvector with eigenvalue 2. Similarly, the y-axis is invariant with eigenvalue 1/2. Now add a shear: B = [[1, 1], [0, 1]]. This takes (x,y) to (x+y, y). The x-axis (y=0) stays fixed: (x,0) -> (x,0). So the x-axis is an invariant line with eigenvalue 1. But the y-axis does not remain: (0,y) -> (y, y), which tilts. So one eigen-direction is obvious (x-axis), the other isn’t, and in fact B has only one eigenvalue 1 with algebraic multiplicity 2 but only one eigenvector direction: it’s a “defective” case.
That shear example confuses me. If it has eigenvalue 1 twice, why isn’t every direction an eigenvector with eigenvalue 1?

Because “eigenvalue repeated” doesn’t mean “everything is invariant.” It means the characteristic polynomial has (t−1)², but the geometric information depends on eigenvectors. For B = [[1,1],[0,1]], the map is: horizontal component gets an added amount proportional to y. If you take a vector with y ≠ 0, it picks up extra x-component, so its direction changes. Only vectors with y = 0 avoid that extra kick. So only the x-axis is invariant. The repeated eigenvalue indicates that, in some coordinate system, the matrix is “almost” the identity but with a shear part. Formally, B = I + N where N = [[0,1],[0,0]] and N² = 0. That N is nilpotent: it pushes vectors sideways but doesn’t have its own eigenvectors except the kernel directions. The eigenvalue 1 captures the “identity-like” scaling, while the nilpotent part captures the shearing that prevents diagonalization.
So eigenvectors are about invariant lines, but what about eigenvalues as “rates” or “strengths”? Is there an intuitive “measurement” interpretation?

Yes: eigenvalues measure how the transformation scales volume along special directions, and more generally how it scales in invariant subspaces. One concrete way: look at repeated application of A. If you apply A again and again to some vector x, you get x, Ax, A²x, A³x, … If x has a component in the direction of an eigenvector v with eigenvalue λ, that component will be multiplied by λ each time. So after k applications, that component scales like λ^k. That makes eigenvalues into growth/decay rates under iteration. Geometrically, you can imagine dropping a cloud of points (say a circle in 2D). Applying A turns the circle into an ellipse (unless there’s shear making it a general ellipse). The principal axes of that ellipse—when A is symmetric—align with eigenvectors, and the lengths of semi-axes are scaled by |eigenvalues|. Even when A isn’t symmetric, singular values play that role more robustly, but eigenvalues still govern iteration and invariant directions.
You mentioned symmetric matrices. Why are they special for intuition?

Because symmetric matrices (real symmetric, or complex Hermitian) have a particularly clean geometry: they represent linear maps that have orthogonal eigenvectors and are diagonalizable with a complete orthonormal basis. That means the map is “pure stretching/compressing” along mutually perpendicular directions, with no rotational mixing between those eigen-directions. If A is symmetric in 2D, it always maps the unit circle to an ellipse whose axes are perpendicular. The eigenvectors point along those axes. The eigenvalues give the signed stretch factors along them. So for symmetric A, eigenvalues are literally the scale factors in an orthogonal coordinate system. For non-symmetric matrices, the image of the unit circle can be an ellipse too, but the axes of that ellipse correspond to singular vectors (SVD), not eigenvectors. Eigenvectors can be non-orthogonal, and the map can combine stretching and rotation and shear in a way that’s harder to visualize.
Can you connect eigenvalues to the determinant and trace in a geometric way?

In 2D (and more generally), there are two classic connections: 1) Determinant as area scaling: det(A) is the factor by which A scales oriented area (in 2D) or volume (in nD). If A has eigenvalues λ1, …, λn (counted with algebraic multiplicity), then det(A) = Π λi. So the product of eigenvalues is the volume scaling. 2) Trace as “total infinitesimal scaling”: tr(A) = sum of eigenvalues. In 2D, if the map is diagonalizable and acts like stretching by λ1 and λ2 along eigen-directions, then trace is λ1 + λ2. It’s not directly an area scaling, but it’s a kind of aggregate measure of expansion: for symmetric matrices, it is the sum of directional stretch factors along an orthonormal basis of eigenvectors, so it’s the sum of principal stretches (with sign). In 2D, you can also interpret trace and determinant as defining the characteristic polynomial: p(λ) = λ² − (tr A) λ + det A. So knowing trace and determinant tells you the eigenvalues (up to order) when they’re real.
I’ve heard “eigenvectors are fixed points on projective space.” What does that mean?

Take the set of all lines through the origin in R^n. That’s projective space: each line corresponds to a “direction,” ignoring magnitude and sign (in real case). A linear map A sends a line ℓ to another line A(ℓ), unless it collapses it to 0. So A induces a map on the space of directions. An eigenvector direction is exactly a line that maps to itself: A(ℓ) = ℓ. In that sense, eigen-directions are fixed points of the induced map on projective space. This viewpoint strips away the scaling factor and focuses on directional behavior. In 2D, projective space is basically a circle of directions with antipodal points identified. A linear transformation acts on this circle of directions. Eigen-directions are fixed points of this action. A rotation by 90° has no fixed direction—consistent with no real eigenvectors.
That’s neat. Can we make it more concrete in 2D with slopes?

Yes. In 2D, a direction can be represented by a slope m = y/x (plus a point at infinity for vertical). Suppose A = [[a,b],[c,d]]. A vector (x, mx) maps to (a x + b mx, c x + d mx) = x (a + bm, c + dm). The new slope m' is: m' = (c + d m) / (a + b m), assuming a + b m ≠ 0. So A acts on slopes by a fractional linear (Möbius) transformation. Eigen-directions correspond to fixed slopes m = m': m = (c + d m) / (a + b m). Rearrange: m(a + b m) = c + d m => a m + b m² = c + d m => b m² + (a − d) m − c = 0. That quadratic gives the eigen-directions (slopes). You can see immediately why you might have 0, 1 (double), or 2 real eigen-directions depending on the discriminant.
If eigenvalues can be complex, what’s the geometric meaning of a complex eigenvalue?

For a real matrix, complex eigenvalues come in conjugate pairs a ± bi. The geometric meaning is: there’s a real invariant 2D subspace (a plane) on which the action is a rotation combined with a uniform scaling. More explicitly, if on some plane the matrix acts like: [x']   [ a  -b ] [x] [y'] = [ b   a ] [y], then the complex eigenvalues are a ± bi. The scaling factor on that plane is r = √(a² + b²), and the rotation angle is θ = atan2(b, a). Every vector in that plane gets rotated by θ and scaled by r. No real direction is preserved unless b=0 (no rotation). So complex eigenvalues indicate “swirling” behavior in some plane rather than pure stretching along a line.
How does this relate to diagonalization? People say eigenvalues are what you see on the diagonal.

Diagonalization is choosing a basis made of eigenvectors. If you can find n linearly independent eigenvectors v1,…,vn, you can form a matrix P with those vectors as columns. In that basis, the linear map acts by: A = P D P^{-1}, where D is diagonal with eigenvalues on the diagonal. Geometrically: changing basis is choosing new coordinate axes. If those axes align with eigen-directions, the transformation becomes “axis-aligned scaling” in that coordinate system: each coordinate scales independently by its eigenvalue. When diagonalization fails (like the shear), you can’t find enough eigen-directions to use as axes. Then the best you can do is Jordan form: blocks that are almost diagonal but with 1s on the superdiagonal, representing shear-like coupling within an invariant subspace.
Can you give me an intuitive feel for Jordan blocks without heavy algebra?

A Jordan block for eigenvalue λ looks like: J = [[λ, 1, 0, ...], [0, λ, 1, ...], ... [0, ..., 0, λ]]. Think of λI as uniform scaling by λ in each coordinate direction. The extra 1s represent a “shift” that pushes some directions into others while still staying within an invariant subspace. In 2D, J = [[λ,1],[0,λ]] acts like: (x,y) -> (λx + y, λy). So y gets scaled by λ, and x gets scaled by λ plus an added contribution from y. This means the line y=0 is still an eigenline (because then x -> λx), but the y-direction leaks into x. Under repeated application, that leakage accumulates in a polynomial way: the y component contributes kλ^{k-1} to x after k steps (if λ≠0). So Jordan blocks correspond to growth that’s not purely exponential λ^k but exponential times a polynomial factor. Geometrically, it’s “scaling plus shear within the same eigenspace.”
When I apply a matrix to a unit circle, I sometimes get an ellipse. Is that related to eigenvalues or singular values? I keep mixing them up.

The image of the unit circle under A is generally an ellipse (or a degenerate ellipse), but the axes of that ellipse are governed by the singular value decomposition (SVD), not eigenvalues, unless A is symmetric (or orthogonally diagonalizable). Singular values σ1 ≥ σ2 ≥ … are the square roots of eigenvalues of A^T A. They represent the maximum and minimum stretch factors of A over all unit vectors: σ1 = max_{||x||=1} ||Ax||, σn = min_{||x||=1} ||Ax||. Eigenvalues instead tell you about invariant directions (Av parallel to v) and iteration behavior. A can have eigenvalues that don’t match the actual maximum stretching of vectors, because eigenvectors might not align with the directions of maximal norm growth for non-normal matrices. So: “unit circle to ellipse axes” is singular values. “directions mapped to themselves” is eigenvectors. When A is symmetric (or more generally normal: A^T A = A A^T), eigenvectors coincide with singular vectors up to scaling, and |eigenvalues| equal singular values.
Can you illustrate a case where eigenvalues are misleading about stretching?

Consider a non-normal matrix like: A = [[1, 100], [0, 1]]. Eigenvalues are both 1. So you might think “no stretching,” but that’s false in norm terms. Apply A to (0,1): you get (100,1), whose length is about 100. So it can stretch some vectors a lot even though eigenvalues are 1. What’s happening: eigenvalues describe long-term behavior under repeated application in an invariant-direction sense. But in the short term, non-normal matrices can have big transient growth because they shear vectors into directions where the norm is large, even if asymptotically there’s no exponential growth (since eigenvalues have magnitude 1). This is one reason people distinguish eigenvalues (spectral radius) from operator norm and singular values.
Okay, but then what is the “true” geometric meaning of eigenvalues if not stretching?

They are “stretching along invariant directions,” and more broadly, they are the intrinsic scalings on invariant subspaces that the map cannot avoid. A good way to reconcile: - Singular values answer: “How much can this map stretch any vector in length, regardless of direction change?” - Eigenvalues answer: “Are there directions that come back to themselves, and how do those components evolve under iteration?” In dynamical terms, if you look at x_{k+1} = A x_k, eigenvalues govern stability. If all eigenvalues satisfy |λ| < 1, then x_k -> 0 for all initial x (when A is diagonalizable or more generally when spectral radius < 1; there are subtleties but that’s the core). If some |λ| > 1, there are directions with exponential growth. So eigenvalues are “asymptotic growth rates along invariant modes.”
What about negative eigenvalues? I see the flip idea, but how does that show up visually?

In 1D, λ < 0 means every step reflects across the origin and scales by |λ|. In 2D with an eigenvector v and eigenvalue λ < 0, vectors along the eigenline reverse direction. If you iterate, the component along that eigenvector alternates sign: after k steps it’s λ^k, so it flips each time if k changes parity. Visually, points on that line jump back and forth across the origin while moving away (if |λ|>1) or toward (if |λ|<1). If you have two real eigenvalues of opposite sign, the transformation reverses orientation (determinant negative), and shapes get mirrored in some way. For instance, a diagonal matrix diag(2, -1) stretches in x and flips+shrinks in y. A square becomes a rectangle but with y inverted.
Can you tie eigenvalues to fixed points of affine transformations? Like x -> Ax + b?

Yes. For the affine map f(x) = Ax + b, fixed points satisfy x = Ax + b, so (I − A)x = b. Whether a unique fixed point exists depends on invertibility of (I − A), i.e., whether 1 is an eigenvalue of A. If 1 is an eigenvalue, then I − A is singular, and you may have no fixed points or infinitely many (depending on b’s alignment with the range). Geometrically: eigenvalue 1 corresponds to a direction that A leaves unchanged (no scaling along that eigenvector). That makes it possible for translations b to “slide” along invariant directions without allowing a unique intersection point.
I want a mental picture for the characteristic polynomial. It feels purely algebraic.

One mental picture: the characteristic polynomial det(A − λI) asks: “For which scalars λ does A − λI fail to be invertible?” That is, for which λ does the transformation A look like “λ times the identity” in at least one direction. A − λI subtracts off uniform scaling by λ in every direction. If after subtracting that, the map collapses some nonzero vector to zero, then that vector was an eigenvector: Av = λv. So eigenvalues are exactly the λ where A and λI intersect in the sense that their difference has a kernel. Geometrically, you can think of sliding λI (uniform scaling) against A and asking when the difference squashes something flat (det=0).
Can you talk about eigenvalues as “resonances” or “modes” without getting into any applications?

Abstractly, a “mode” is a shape that reproduces itself under the transformation, up to scaling. If you decompose a vector x into components along eigenvectors (when possible), each component evolves independently under A: the v_i component just gets multiplied by λ_i. So eigenvectors are the shapes that don’t mix with others; eigenvalues tell how strongly that mode is scaled. Even without any external interpretation, this is a structural statement: eigen-decomposition is a way of splitting the space into invariant subspaces where the map is as simple as possible.
What about complex eigenvectors? How do I picture a complex eigenvector if I’m staying in real space?

For a real matrix with complex eigenvalues, it’s often better to picture the real invariant plane rather than the complex eigenvector itself. If v is a complex eigenvector for λ = a + bi, then its real and imaginary parts, Re(v) and Im(v), span a real 2D invariant subspace. On that plane, the action is rotation+scaling. So instead of picturing a complex direction, picture a plane with a spiral similarity transform: every vector rotates by θ and scales by r. The complex eigenvector is a compact way to encode that behavior.
Is there a way to detect from the matrix whether it will rotate vs stretch, without solving for eigenvalues?

In 2D, you can use trace and determinant. For A real 2×2, the discriminant of the characteristic polynomial is: Δ = (tr A)² − 4 det A. - If Δ > 0, eigenvalues are real and distinct: there are two real eigen-directions (stretching along two lines, though possibly with shearing if not symmetric). - If Δ = 0, repeated real eigenvalue: one or two eigen-directions depending on diagonalizability. - If Δ < 0, complex conjugate pair: no real eigen-directions; behavior includes rotation in the plane. Also, det(A) > 0 vs < 0 tells orientation preservation vs reversal.
Can we discuss invariant subspaces more generally? Eigenvectors are 1D invariant subspaces, but what about higher dimensional ones?

An invariant subspace W is a subspace such that A(W) ⊆ W. Eigenvectors give 1D invariant subspaces. But you can have invariant planes, etc. For example, if A is block diagonal: A = [[B, 0], [0, C]], then the coordinates split into two invariant subspaces corresponding to the blocks. Even if B has no real eigenvectors (e.g., a rotation), the 2D subspace for that block is invariant. More generally, Jordan blocks produce invariant chains: span{v} ⊂ span{v, w} ⊂ … where A acts in a nested way. Invariant subspaces are the “pieces” you can restrict the transformation to without mixing with the rest. Eigenvalues label these invariant pieces: the generalized eigenspace for λ is the set of vectors killed by (A − λI)^k for some k. That space is invariant and captures all the Jordan structure associated with λ.
You said earlier that eigenvalues govern iteration and stability. Can you make that purely geometric, like what happens to shapes under repeated application?

Imagine applying A repeatedly to a circle (or sphere). Under one application, it becomes some ellipse-like shape. Under repeated application, it will tend to elongate along directions associated with eigenvalues of largest magnitude (if A is diagonalizable and has a dominant eigenvalue magnitude). More precisely: if A has eigenvalues with magnitudes |λ1| > |λ2| ≥ … and a corresponding eigenvector v1, then for a generic starting vector x (not orthogonal to the left eigenvector in a certain sense), A^k x points increasingly in the direction of v1, because the λ1^k component dominates. So the shape of A^k applied to a set tends to align with the dominant eigen-direction: everything gets “pulled” toward that direction in projective space. If the dominant eigenvalues are complex with largest magnitude, you get spiraling behavior in the dominant invariant plane.
“Generic starting vector” sounds like it hides something. When does it fail?

It fails when the initial vector has zero component in the dominant eigen-direction (or more accurately, lies in an invariant subspace that excludes it).
