For a diagonalizable matrix with basis of eigenvectors, if x has coefficient 0 on v1, then the next largest eigenvalue governs. If x lies entirely in a subspace spanned by other eigenvectors, it will never develop a v1 component because that subspace is invariant. For defective matrices, components can leak via Jordan chains, but still, the asymptotics are governed by eigenvalues with maximal magnitude, with polynomial factors in k if Jordan blocks are involved.
Can you explain left eigenvectors intuitively? I usually ignore them.

Right eigenvectors satisfy Av = λv. Left eigenvectors satisfy w^T A = λ w^T (or A^T w = λ w). Geometrically, right eigenvectors are invariant directions in the space of vectors. Left eigenvectors correspond to invariant directions in the dual space (linear functionals, or hyperplanes). A left eigenvector w defines a linear functional φ(x) = w^T x. The condition w^T A = λ w^T means: φ(Ax) = λ φ(x). So measuring x with φ, then applying A, scales that measurement by λ. In other words, w picks out an “observable” (a linear measurement) that scales simply under A. In 2D, w corresponds to a family of parallel lines (level sets w^T x = const). Under A, those level sets get mapped so that the spacing between them scales by λ in a consistent way.
That dual-space picture is interesting. Does it connect to projections or decompositions?

Yes. If A is diagonalizable with distinct eigenvalues, you can form spectral projectors: P_i = v_i w_i^T / (w_i^T v_i), where v_i is a right eigenvector and w_i a corresponding left eigenvector normalized so w_i^T v_i = 1. Then P_i is the projection onto the i-th eigen-direction along the others (not necessarily orthogonal). These projectors satisfy: P_i^2 = P_i, P_i P_j = 0 (i≠j), and sum P_i = I, and A = Σ λ_i P_i. Geometrically, it’s decomposing the space into invariant one-dimensional components, but the notion of “component” is defined using left eigenvectors as the coordinate functionals.
Can we do a concrete 2×2 example where left and right eigenvectors differ and show what it means?

Take A = [[2, 1], [0, 1]]. Eigenvalues are 2 and 1. Right eigenvector for λ=2: solve (A−2I)v=0 gives [[0,1],[0,-1]] v = 0, so y=0, v2 = (1,0). Right eigenvector for λ=1: (A−I) = [[1,1],[0,0]], so x = −y, v1 = (−1,1). Now left eigenvectors: solve A^T w = λ w. A^T = [[2,0],[1,1]]. For λ=2: (A^T−2I) = [[0,0],[1,-1]], so w satisfies w1 = w2. Choose w2 = (1,1). For λ=1: (A^T−I) = [[1,0],[1,0]], so w1=0, choose w1 = (0,1). Interpretation: - The right eigenvector (1,0) is an invariant direction in the plane. - The left eigenvector (1,1) defines the functional x+y. Under A, x+y scales by 2 when restricted appropriately: (1,1)^T A = 2(1,1)^T. - The left eigenvector (0,1) is the functional “take y.” Under A, y is unchanged: y' = y, consistent with eigenvalue 1. You can see right eigenvectors tell you invariant lines in the domain; left eigenvectors tell you invariant families of parallel lines in the codomain/dual sense.
I want to understand the relationship between eigenvalues and “angles.” When do eigenvectors end up orthogonal?

Eigenvectors end up orthogonal when the matrix is symmetric (or more generally normal: A^T A = A A^T in real case, or A* A = A A* in complex). Normal matrices are exactly those that can be unitarily diagonalized, meaning there’s an orthonormal basis of eigenvectors. Geometrically, normality means the map doesn’t have “one-sided shear” that creates non-orthogonal eigenvectors; it behaves like a combination of rotations and scalings in mutually orthogonal invariant subspaces. If A is not normal, eigenvectors can be very non-orthogonal, even almost parallel, which is tied to sensitivity of eigenvalues and transient growth phenomena.
Sensitivity of eigenvalues? Can you describe that geometrically?

If eigenvectors are nearly linearly dependent (almost parallel), then small perturbations of A can cause large changes in eigenvalues. One geometric reason: diagonalization A = P D P^{-1}. If P is ill-conditioned (columns nearly dependent), then small changes in A correspond to relatively larger changes in D in that coordinate system. In the plane, imagine two eigen-directions that are very close. Then the coordinate system built from them is skewed; representing vectors in that basis requires large coefficients that cancel. Small perturbations can change the invariant directions a lot, which changes eigenvalues. This is again tied to non-normality. Normal matrices have orthonormal eigenvectors, giving stable spectral structure.
Can we revisit the “projective fixed points” idea but for 3D? Is there a way to picture it?

In 3D, directions correspond to points on the unit sphere with antipodal points identified (since v and −v represent the same line). A linear map A sends a direction (a point on this sphere) to another direction by mapping the vector and re-normalizing. Eigen-directions are fixed points of this induced map on the sphere (mod antipodes). Visually, if you imagine many points on the sphere and apply A then normalize, the flow of points on the sphere tends to move toward dominant eigen-directions (attractors) or away (repellors), depending on eigenvalue magnitudes. If there’s a pair of complex eigenvalues, you get an invariant plane and an orthogonal real eigen-direction (if the matrix is real and has one real eigenvalue). Then on the sphere, points tend to rotate around the real eigen-axis while being attracted/repelled depending on scaling factors.
I’ve heard of the spectral theorem. Without stating it formally, what’s the geometric essence?

The geometric essence (for real symmetric matrices) is: any such transformation can be understood as stretching space along orthogonal axes. There is a set of perpendicular directions (eigenvectors) such that applying the matrix just scales each coordinate independently along those directions. So symmetric matrices are exactly those linear maps that have no “twist” relative to the Euclidean inner product: they can be fully described by perpendicular principal axes and stretch factors.
Does that connect to quadratic forms? Like x^T A x?

Very directly. For symmetric A, the scalar x^T A x defines a quadratic form. The level sets x^T A x = 1 (when A is positive definite) are ellipsoids. The eigenvectors give the principal axes of those ellipsoids; the eigenvalues determine the squared reciprocals of axis lengths (depending on convention). Specifically, if A is symmetric positive definite and A = Q Λ Q^T with Q orthogonal and Λ diagonal with positive entries λ_i, then x^T A x = 1 becomes: y^T Λ y = 1 with y = Q^T x, i.e., Σ λ_i y_i^2 = 1. So along the i-th eigen-direction, the intercept is 1/√λ_i. Bigger λ means tighter curvature (shorter axis). This gives a very geometric meaning: eigenvalues describe curvature/shape of quadratic bowls and ellipsoids.
That’s a strong intuition. But for a general matrix, x^T A x isn’t even symmetric. What then?

For any real A, x^T A x depends only on the symmetric part S = (A + A^T)/2, because: x^T A x = x^T S x (since x^T (A − A^T) x = 0 for skew-symmetric part). So quadratic forms inherently “see” only the symmetric part. If you want geometry for general A, you often separate A into symmetric + skew-symmetric: A = S + K, where S^T = S and K^T = −K. Then: - S corresponds to pure stretching/compressing along orthogonal axes (in 2D/3D). - K corresponds to an infinitesimal rotation (in 2D, K is like [[0, −ω],[ω, 0]]). This decomposition is very geometric: any linear map can be thought of as “stretch + rotate,” but note this is not the same as polar decomposition (which is global rotation times symmetric stretch). Still, it helps build intuition.
Polar decomposition—how does that relate to eigenvalues?

Polar decomposition says A = Q H where Q is orthogonal (a rotation/reflection) and H is symmetric positive semidefinite. H captures the pure stretching, Q captures the rotation/reflection. The eigenvalues of H are the singular values of A. So polar decomposition is closely tied to singular values, not eigenvalues. Eigenvalues of A itself mix rotation and stretching. For normal matrices, eigenvalues’ magnitudes equal singular values and their arguments encode rotation angles in invariant planes. For non-normal matrices, eigenvalues don’t fully describe stretching; polar decomposition gives a clearer geometric split, but it uses singular values. So eigenvalues are not “the” geometry; they’re the geometry of invariant modes and iteration, while polar/SVD is the geometry of metric distortion.
If eigenvalues are about invariant modes, can we interpret them as “solutions” to some optimization?

For symmetric matrices, yes: the Rayleigh quotient: R(x) = (x^T A x) / (x^T x). For symmetric A, the maximum of R(x) over nonzero x is the largest eigenvalue, and the minimizer is the smallest eigenvalue. Moreover, the vectors achieving these extrema are eigenvectors. Geometrically, R(x) measures how the quadratic form compares to the norm squared along direction x. The extremal directions are principal axes. For non-symmetric matrices, the Rayleigh quotient doesn’t have the same clean eigenvalue extremal property (it relates to the field of values and can be complex), so the optimization interpretation is special to symmetric/Hermitian.
Can you describe the “field of values” (numerical range) geometrically, and how it differs from eigenvalues?

The numerical range of A is: W(A) = { x* A x : ||x|| = 1 }. For complex matrices, this is a subset of the complex plane. For real matrices, it’s typically an interval or region in the complex plane when viewed complexly. Key geometry: - For Hermitian A, W(A) is the real interval [λ_min, λ_max]. So it collapses to eigenvalues’ convex hull on the real line. - For general A, W(A) is a convex set in the complex plane (Toeplitz–Hausdorff theorem). It contains the eigenvalues, but can be much larger. So eigenvalues are discrete points; numerical range is a filled-in convex region describing all possible “quadratic measurements” x*Ax. It captures non-normality: for highly non-normal A, W(A) can be large even if eigenvalues are small.
I’m trying to reconcile all these objects: eigenvalues, singular values, numerical range. What’s a clean mental map?

A compact mental map: 1) Eigenvalues/eigenvectors: - Invariant modes: Av = λv. - Govern iteration asymptotics and invariant subspaces. - For normal matrices, they also directly describe geometric rotation+scaling on orthogonal subspaces. 2) Singular values/SVD: - Metric distortion: how lengths and volumes change. - Image of unit sphere becomes ellipsoid; axes lengths are singular values. - Always well-behaved, real nonnegative. 3) Numerical range: - Quadratic measurements x*Ax. - Convex region containing eigenvalues; reflects non-normality. - For Hermitian, equals interval between extreme eigenvalues. If you want “direction preserved,” think eigenvectors. If you want “maximum stretch,” think singular values. If you want “what values can x*Ax take,” think numerical range.
Back to eigenvalues: is there a way to see them as “solutions to a self-consistency equation” geometrically?

Yes: Av is parallel to v. That’s a self-consistency requirement: the direction you start with must be the direction you end with. In 2D slope form, that was m = (c+dm)/(a+bm), a fixed point equation. In higher dimensions, “parallel” means the cross product-like condition that all 2×2 minors of [v, Av] vanish (they’re linearly dependent). That’s equivalent to rank([A−λI]) < n for some λ. So eigenvalues arise when there is a nontrivial direction that is self-consistent under A. You can also see it as: the map from directions to directions has fixed points; eigen-directions are those fixed points; eigenvalues are the corresponding scaling factors.
Can we talk about complex arguments of eigenvalues as “rotation angles”? Is that always valid?

It’s valid cleanly for normal matrices (including rotations/scalings) and for 2×2 real blocks corresponding to complex conjugate pairs. If A has an eigenvalue λ = r e^{iθ} with eigenvector v in a complex vector space, then applying A to v multiplies it by r and rotates its complex phase by θ. But translating that into real geometric rotation is straightforward only when you have a real invariant 2D subspace where A acts like the matrix [[a, -b],[b, a]]. For non-normal matrices, even with complex eigenvalues, the action on real vectors can have additional shearing. The “angle” is still meaningful as the argument of eigenvalues in the complex plane, but it doesn’t always correspond to a pure rotation in an orthogonal plane in the real space unless the matrix is normal or has that canonical real block structure.
I’d like a more tangible 2D example with complex eigenvalues. Give me a matrix and explain what it does to vectors.

Take: A = [[0, -2], [1,  0]]. This maps (x,y) to (−2y, x). It’s like a rotation by 90° combined with anisotropic scaling because the coefficients differ. Compute its effect on a vector: (1,0) -> (0,1). (0,1) -> (−2,0). So the unit x-axis goes to unit y-axis; unit y-axis goes to length-2 negative x-axis. Apply twice: (1,0) -> (0,1) -> (−2,0). So after two steps, you’re scaled by 2 and flipped. That suggests eigenvalues with magnitude √2 and angle ±45°? Let’s check quickly: characteristic polynomial is λ² + 2 = 0? Actually det([[−λ, −2],[1, −λ]]) = λ² + 2. So eigenvalues are ± i√2. That’s magnitude √2, angle ±90°. On the plane, the action is a rotation by 90° combined with scaling by √2, but in a skewed coordinate sense? Here it’s actually similar to √2 times a rotation by 90° after an appropriate change of basis? More precisely, this A is not orthogonal times scalar, but it still has pure imaginary eigenvalues indicating rotation-like behavior with scaling. If you want the clean rotation+scaling form, use: B = [[a, -b], [b,  a]]. For example a=1, b=2: B = [[1, -2], [2,  1]]. This maps every vector by rotating it by atan2(2,1) and scaling by √(1²+2²)=√5. Here eigenvalues are 1±2i, exactly encoding that.
That last one is clearer. So for B, every vector gets rotated by the same angle? That’s surprisingly simple.

Yes—because B corresponds to multiplying complex numbers by (a+bi). If you identify (x,y) with x+iy, then multiplying by (1+2i) scales by √5 and rotates by θ = arctan(2). That action is uniform for all vectors: same rotation angle, same scaling. This is a special subclass of 2×2 real matrices: those that represent complex multiplication (they commute with the 90° rotation matrix). For general 2×2 matrices with complex eigenvalues, you still get rotation+scaling after a change of basis, but not necessarily in the standard Euclidean geometry.
I think I’m starting to see. But when people say “eigenvalues are the axes of an ellipse,” that seems wrong unless symmetric. How would you correct that statement?

I’d rephrase it: - For a symmetric matrix A, eigenvectors are the axes of the ellipse obtained from the unit circle under A (more precisely, under the quadratic form or under A acting on the circle, because symmetry aligns things). - For a general matrix A, the axes of the ellipse A maps the unit circle to are given by the singular vectors, and the axis lengths are singular values. So the “axes of ellipse” story belongs to SVD, not eigen-decomposition, unless A is symmetric/normal. Eigenvalues still have a geometric role, but it’s about invariant directions and asymptotics rather than instantaneous metric distortion.
Could you connect eigenvalues to “principal directions” in some other geometric object, if not the ellipse?

Yes: for any linear map A, eigenvectors are principal directions of the induced action on projective space (directions), and for symmetric A, they are principal directions of the quadratic form x^T A x and the corresponding ellipsoids. For general A, eigenvectors are the directions where the action is purely radial (no tangential component on the sphere after normalization). If you take the unit sphere and consider the vector field that sends a point u to the normalized direction of Au, eigenvectors correspond to fixed points of that field. That’s a geometric object: a map on the sphere (or projective sphere). So instead of “ellipse axes,” think “fixed directions on the sphere of directions.”
How does this relate to the idea of “diagonal entries are what happens to basis vectors”? That’s also a common intuition.

Matrix columns tell you where basis vectors go: the j-th column of A is A e_j. Diagonal entries are just the components of those images along the original basis vectors. But eigenvalues are about finding a basis where each basis vector maps to a scalar multiple of itself. So diagonal entries in an arbitrary basis don’t have intrinsic meaning—they depend on your coordinate choice. Eigenvalues are basis-invariant (up to ordering), and they are the diagonal entries in the special eigenbasis (when it exists). Geometrically: diagonal entries are “what happens to coordinate axes you chose.” Eigenvalues are “what happens to the intrinsic invariant axes determined by the transformation.”
Can you show me a transformation that has obvious eigenvectors but not aligned with standard axes?

Take a projection onto a line. Let u be a unit vector in direction (1,1)/√2. The orthogonal projection matrix is: P = u u^T = (1/2) [[1,1],[1,1]]. Geometrically, P takes every vector and drops it perpendicularly onto the line y=x. The line y=x is invariant: vectors on it stay the same, so eigenvalue 1 with eigenvector (1,1). The perpendicular line y=−x collapses to 0, so eigenvalue 0 with eigenvector (1,−1). In the standard basis, P isn’t diagonal, but eigenvectors are obvious from the geometry: “along the line” and “perpendicular to the line.”
That example is great. So eigenvalues 1 and 0 correspond to “kept” and “killed” components.

Exactly. Any projection has eigenvalues 0 and 1. Eigenvalue 1 corresponds to the subspace you keep; eigenvalue 0 corresponds to the subspace you annihilate. That’s a very geometric class of examples. Similarly, reflections have eigenvalues 1 and −1: vectors in the mirror line are unchanged (1), vectors perpendicular are flipped (−1).
What about a rank-1 update like A = I + u v^T? Can eigenvalues be understood geometrically there?

Yes, to some extent. A = I + u v^T maps x to x + u (v^T x). It takes the scalar “measurement” v^T x and adds that times u. So it’s identity plus a push in the u direction proportional to how much x aligns with v. Geometrically, most directions are unchanged except they get an added component along u. The eigen-structure often includes: - Any vector orthogonal to v (i.e., v^T x = 0) is fixed: Ax = x, so eigenvalue 1 on the (n−1)-dimensional subspace v^⊥. - There may be one additional eigenvalue 1 + v^T u with eigenvector in the span of u plus possibly something else, depending on alignment. So you get a big invariant subspace with eigenvalue 1 and one special direction that gets scaled differently.
That’s a nice pattern. It sounds like eigenvalues are easiest when the transformation has some clear invariant subspaces.

Yes. The heart of eigen-intuition is invariance: identify subspaces that the map doesn’t mix with the rest. Then within each invariant piece, the map may reduce to scaling (diagonal) or scaling+shear (Jordan) or rotation+scaling (complex pair). When invariant subspaces are not obvious, computation finds them, but the geometric meaning is still “these are the directions/subspaces the map respects.”
I want to test my understanding: if a matrix has eigenvalue 0, that means it squashes something to zero along an eigenvector direction. Is that equivalent to being singular?

Exactly. λ=0 is an eigenvalue iff det(A)=0 iff A is singular. Geometrically, singular means the map collapses volume to zero: it squashes the space into a lower-dimensional subset. The kernel is a nontrivial subspace, and every nonzero vector in the kernel is an eigenvector with eigenvalue 0. So 0 eigenvalue means there are directions that get completely annihilated.
If I have a matrix with all eigenvalues positive, does that mean it preserves orientation and doesn’t flip any directions?

If all eigenvalues are positive real, then the determinant (product) is positive, so it preserves orientation (assuming real matrix). But “doesn’t flip any directions” is subtler: eigenvalues positive means along eigenvectors there’s no sign flip. But if the matrix isn’t diagonalizable over reals, or eigenvectors aren’t a full basis, you can still have shears. Still, no eigen-direction experiences a reversal. If a matrix has complex eigenvalues, the notion of “flip” isn’t directly about eigenvalues’ sign. Orientation is determined by det(A): negative det implies orientation reversal. A matrix can have positive determinant even with some negative eigenvalues, as long as an even number are negative.
Could you give me a purely geometric way to see determinant equals product of eigenvalues?

One way is: if A is diagonalizable, then in an eigenbasis it’s diagonal with entries λ_i. The determinant of a diagonal matrix is the product of diagonal entries, and determinant is basis-invariant, so det(A)=Π λ_i. Geometrically: in that eigenbasis, the map scales the unit cube into a box with side lengths |λ_i| along each axis (with sign encoding orientation flips). The volume scaling is product of side scalings. For non-diagonalizable matrices, you can use continuity: small perturbations make it diagonalizable generically, and determinant and eigenvalues vary continuously (with some caveats), and the identity det=product of eigenvalues still holds algebraically in general.
Since you mentioned continuity, can eigenvalues change continuously with the matrix?

As a set (multiset), eigenvalues depend continuously on matrix entries in a certain sense, but individual eigenvalues can swap and can be non-smooth at repeated eigenvalues. For non-normal matrices, eigenvalues can be highly sensitive. Geometrically, at a repeated eigenvalue, the invariant directions can change abruptly under tiny perturbations. This is again tied to eigenvector conditioning and the geometry of nearly defective matrices.
If I want intuition for “why diagonalization matters,” can you give a geometric story that isn’t about simplifying computation?

Diagonalization is about decoupling. If a transformation is diagonalizable, the space splits into independent one-dimensional modes. Each mode scales independently, and nothing mixes. That gives a complete geometric description: you know exactly how any vector evolves by projecting onto these modes. If not diagonalizable, modes are coupled within invariant subspaces: a component in one generalized direction feeds into another under the map (shear). Geometrically, you can’t find a coordinate system that makes the transformation purely “separate scalings”—there’s intrinsic coupling. So diagonalization is a statement about the intrinsic geometry of the transformation: whether it can be represented as pure scaling along some axes.
Can we go deeper into the “coupling” idea with a 3D Jordan block example?

Consider a 3×3 Jordan block with eigenvalue λ: J = [[λ, 1, 0], [0, λ, 1], [0, 0, λ]]. In coordinates (x,y,z): (x',y',z') = (λx + y, λy + z, λz). You have an eigenline along the x-axis? Actually eigenvectors satisfy (J−λI)v=0, which implies y=0 and z=0, so only the x-axis is eigen. But there’s an invariant plane spanned by x and y (set z=0 stays z'=0), and the whole space is invariant. Geometrically: - z scales by λ. - y scales by λ but also receives input from z. - x scales by λ but also receives input from y. So z “leaks” into y, and y leaks into x. Under iteration, the influence of z on x accumulates with a k^2 factor times λ^{k-2}, etc. It’s like a cascade of shears layered atop scaling. This is why Jordan blocks correspond to polynomial times exponential behavior under iteration.
Does this coupling show up if I look at A^k explicitly?

Yes. For J = λI + N with N nilpotent (N^3=0 here), you can use the binomial theorem: J^k = (λI + N)^k = Σ_{j=0}^{2} C(k,j) λ^{k-j} N^j, since N^3=0 kills higher terms. So you get terms like k λ^{k-1} N and (k choose 2) λ^{k-2} N^2. That’s the polynomial factor. Geometrically, N represents shear steps; repeated application accumulates those shears in a combinatorial way.
I’m curious: is there a purely geometric criterion for diagonalizability?

A geometric phrasing: A is diagonalizable over a field if the space decomposes into a direct sum of invariant one-dimensional subspaces (lines) spanning the space. Equivalently, you can find enough independent eigen-directions to form a basis. Another geometric criterion: for each eigenvalue λ, the dimension of the eigenspace (the set of vectors fixed in direction by A with scaling λ) must equal the multiplicity of λ as a root of the characteristic polynomial. When it’s smaller, you have “missing” eigen-directions, and the extra structure appears as shear within the generalized eigenspace. So diagonalizability is about whether the invariant subspaces associated to each λ split fully into independent lines rather than having inseparable shear coupling.
What about complex diagonalization vs real? Like a real matrix might not be diagonalizable over reals but is over complex?

Exactly. A real rotation matrix in 2D with angle not 0 or π has complex eigenvalues e^{±iθ} and is diagonalizable over C, but not over R because there are no real eigenvectors. Geometrically, over R you can’t split the plane into invariant lines; the smallest invariant piece is the whole plane, where the action is rotation. Over C you can treat it as scaling along complex eigen-directions, which is algebraically convenient but less directly visual in real space. So the “right” field determines what invariant subspaces you can break into.
Is there a way to visualize complex eigenvectors in terms of real spirals?

Yes. Suppose A has eigenvalues r e^{±iθ} with r≠0. Then on the invariant plane, iterating A sends a vector to r^k times a rotation by kθ: a spiral (outward if r>1, inward if r<1) in that plane. That’s a real picture of complex eigenvalues: spiraling dynamics. Even without iteration, a single application rotates by θ and scales by r. The complex exponential captures both in one number.
I’ve also seen “eigenvalues are the roots of the characteristic polynomial.” Is there a geometric interpretation of that polynomial itself, maybe in terms of volumes?

The characteristic polynomial p(λ)=det(A−λI) can be viewed as the oriented volume scaling of the map (A−λI). For each λ, A−λI is a linear map; its determinant is how it scales volume. Eigenvalues are exactly where that volume scaling becomes zero—where the map collapses space in some direction. In 2D, det(A−λI)=0 means the parallelogram formed by (A−λI)e1 and (A−λI)e2 has zero area, i.e., those images are linearly dependent. That dependence corresponds to the existence of a nonzero v such that (A−λI)v=0. So the characteristic polynomial is a “volume collapse function” in λ.
That’s pretty satisfying. If I were to summarize eigenvalues in one sentence geometrically, what would it be?

Eigenvalues are the scaling factors associated with invariant directions (or invariant subspaces) of a linear transformation—directions that the transformation maps onto themselves, governing the simplest decoupled behavior and the asymptotic growth/rotation under iteration.
I want to push one more subtlety: if eigenvectors are invariant directions, why do we sometimes get eigenvectors that are not orthogonal and seem “non-geometric”?

Because “invariant” is an algebraic condition, not a metric one. Orthogonality depends on an inner product (a notion of angle). A general linear map can distort angles and doesn’t have to respect the inner product. So its invariant directions need not be perpendicular. When A is symmetric/normal, it respects the inner product structure enough that invariant directions can be chosen orthogonal. When it’s not, invariant directions can be skew. So non-orthogonal eigenvectors are still geometric—just in the sense of invariant lines—without being “nice” relative to Euclidean angles.
If I change the inner product, can I make eigenvectors orthogonal?

Sometimes. If A is diagonalizable with real eigenvalues, you can often choose an inner product with respect to which the eigenvectors become orthogonal (by declaring them orthonormal in that new inner product). But that inner product may be very different from the standard one; it essentially bakes the eigenbasis into the geometry. For a matrix with complex eigenvalues or defective structure, you can’t generally make it orthogonally diagonalizable by changing inner product in a simple way; orthogonal diagonalization is tied to normality with respect to the chosen inner product. So you can “force” orthogonality by redefining geometry, but whether that feels natural depends on what structure you want to preserve.
This has been very conceptual, which I wanted. Could we end with a mental exercise I can do: given a transformation, how to hunt for eigenvectors geometrically?

Here are a few geometric hunting strategies: 1) Look for invariant subspaces: - Does the map obviously keep a line/plane fixed (like projections, reflections, block structure, triangular matrices often preserve coordinate subspaces)? 2) Track what happens to a family of lines: - In 2D, pick a line through origin, see where it maps. If a line maps to itself, that’s an eigen-direction. Using slopes: solve fixed slope m = (c+dm)/(a+bm). 3) Use iteration on directions: - Pick a random vector x, repeatedly apply A and renormalize. If there’s a dominant real eigenvalue in magnitude and the matrix is reasonably behaved, the direction tends to converge to the dominant eigenvector direction. 4) Use symmetry: - If A is symmetric, eigenvectors align with principal axes of the quadratic form x^T A x or of the ellipsoid defined by it. 5) For complex behavior: - If 2D and trace^2 < 4 det, expect no real eigenvectors; instead look for rotation+scaling behavior in the plane. If you do these, computation becomes a confirmation rather than the whole story.
Thanks. I think I finally have an internal picture of what eigenvalues are doing.
I still don’t buy that switching doors gives you 2/3 probability. Can you convince me?

Let’s pin down the exact rules, because most objections come from implicitly changing them. Standard Monty Hall rules: 1) There are 3 doors. Exactly 1 has a prize, 2 have goats. 2) You choose 1 door (no information yet). 3) The host (Monty) knows where the prize is. 4) Monty must open one of the two doors you didn’t choose, and he must open a goat door (never the prize). 5) If both unchosen doors are goats, Monty chooses which goat door to open by some fixed rule (could be random, could be “always leftmost,” etc.), but crucially he still always opens a goat. 6) After opening a goat door, Monty offers you the option to switch to the remaining unopened door. Under these rules, switching wins with probability 2/3. If you want, tell me which of the rules you disagree with or think are missing.
I accept those rules. But after he opens a goat, there are two doors left. Why wouldn’t it be 1/2 each at that point?

Because “two doors left” doesn’t mean “symmetric information.” The two remaining doors are not equivalent: one is the door you initially chose (which was chosen with no information), and the other is a door that survived Monty’s filtering process (Monty deliberately avoided the prize). A clean way to see it without handwaving is to compute conditional probabilities. Label doors A, B, C. You pick A. Before anything happens: P(prize behind A) = 1/3 P(prize behind B) = 1/3 P(prize behind C) = 1/3 Now Monty opens one of B or C, but he is constrained: he must open a goat. That means Monty’s action depends on where the prize is, so Monty’s action conveys information. Case analysis: - If prize is behind A (prob 1/3): Monty can open either B or C (both goats). You switch, you lose. - If prize is behind B (prob 1/3): Monty cannot open B; he must open C (goat). You switch to B, you win. - If prize is behind C (prob 1/3): Monty must open B. You switch to C, you win. Switching wins in the last two cases: 2/3. The 1/2 intuition assumes Monty opened a door “at random” without knowing where the prize is, or that he might accidentally reveal the prize. But he doesn’t.

But you used a case analysis that feels like it’s just restating the claim. I want a more formal conditional probability argument. Like Bayes’ theorem. Show me why P(prize behind my original door | Monty opened some goat door) stays 1/3.

Sure. We’ll do it with explicit events and Bayes. Assume you choose A. Suppose Monty opens door B and reveals a goat. Let’s compute: P(prize behind A | Monty opens B) Define events: - A_prize: prize behind A - B_prize: prize behind B - C_prize: prize behind C - M_opens_B: Monty opens B (and it’s a goat, but under the rules Monty never opens the prize anyway, so “opens B” implies it’s a goat unless prize is behind B, which can’t happen if he opens B) We need P(A_prize | M_opens_B). By Bayes: P(A_prize | M_opens_B) = P(M_opens_B | A_prize) * P(A_prize) / P(M_opens_B) We know P(A_prize)=1/3. Now compute P(M_opens_B | A_prize). If prize behind A, Monty can open either B or C. We need Monty’s tie-breaking rule. Suppose when he has a choice, he chooses uniformly at random. Then: P(M_opens_B | A_prize) = 1/2. Next P(M_opens_B | B_prize) = 0 because Monty cannot open B if prize behind B. P(M_opens_B | C_prize) = 1 because if prize behind C, Monty must open B (since B is the only unchosen goat). Now total probability: P(M_opens_B) = P(M_opens_B|A_prize)P(A_prize) + P(M_opens_B|B_prize)P(B_prize) + P(M_opens_B|C_prize)P(C_prize) = (1/2)(1/3) + (0)(1/3) + (1)(1/3) = 1/6 + 0 + 1/3 = 1/2. Therefore: P(A_prize | M_opens_B) = [(1/2)(1/3)] / (1/2) = (1/6)/(1/2) = 1/3. And the remaining unopened door is C, so: P(C_prize | M_opens_B) = 1 - 1/3 = 2/3. That’s Bayes: your original door stays at 1/3; the other unopened door becomes 2/3.
You assumed he chooses randomly when he has a choice. What if Monty has some bias? Then does switching still give 2/3?

Good question. There are two different claims: 1) P(your original door has prize | Monty opened a goat door) = 1/3 under very broad conditions. 2) P(switch wins | Monty opened specific door B) can depend on Monty’s bias in the “both goats” case, but switching is still at least as good as staying, and often still 2/3 depending on what you condition on. Let’s separate “unconditional switching advantage” from “conditional on which door Monty opened.” Unconditional: If the rules are “Monty always opens a goat door and always offers a switch,” then switching wins with probability 2/3 regardless of his bias. Reason: switching wins exactly when your initial pick was wrong, which happens with probability 2/3. Monty’s bias doesn’t change whether your initial pick was right or wrong. Conditional on which door he opened: If you condition on a specific opened door, the posterior can depend on Monty’s tie-break rule, because the probability of him opening B vs C when you were initially correct can be skewed. But if you don’t condition on which door he opened—only that he opened a goat and offered switching—then switching is 2/3.
Wait, I am conditioning on which door he opened because I see it. So doesn’t that matter? Like if he opens B, maybe that gives extra information that changes the odds away from 2/3.

It can, in some variants. Let’s compute with a general bias. Assume you picked A. Prize location equally likely among A,B,C. If prize behind A, Monty has a choice: open B with probability q and open C with probability 1−q. If prize behind B, Monty must open C. If prize behind C, Monty must open B. Now you observe Monty opens B. Compute P(C_prize | M_opens_B). First compute P(M_opens_B): - If A_prize: probability q - If B_prize: probability 0 (he must open C) - If C_prize: probability 1 (he must open B) So: P(M_opens_B) = (1/3)q + (1/3)0 + (1/3)1 = (q+1)/3. Now: P(C_prize | M_opens_B) = P(M_opens_B | C_prize)P(C_prize) / P(M_opens_B) = (1)(1/3) / ((q+1)/3) = 1/(q+1). Similarly: P(A_prize | M_opens_B) = P(M_opens_B | A_prize)P(A_prize) / P(M_opens_B) = q(1/3) / ((q+1)/3) = q/(q+1). And B_prize posterior is 0 because Monty opened B. So after seeing Monty open B, the remaining unopened door is C, and switching gives probability 1/(q+1). If q=1/2, that’s 2/3. If q is very small (Monty almost never opens B when he has a choice), then seeing him open B is strong evidence you were not correct, making switching even better: if q→0, switching→1. If q is very large (Monty almost always opens B when he has a choice), then seeing him open B provides less evidence against you; switching becomes 1/(1+q), which approaches 1/2 as q→1. So yes, conditioning on which door he opened can change the posterior if Monty’s behavior is biased and known to you. But the classic statement “switching gives 2/3” assumes either symmetry (q=1/2) or that you’re not using the identity of the opened door for extra inference (or equivalently, Monty chooses uniformly when indifferent).
That’s interesting. So if q=1, switching after he opens B gives 1/2? That sounds like it supports my intuition.

In that particular conditional scenario, yes, switching can be 1/2. But note what “q=1” means: when you initially picked the prize (A_prize), Monty always opens B, never C. So observing “Monty opened B” becomes less informative because it happens both when you’re correct (with certainty) and when the prize is behind C (also with certainty). Those two cases become equally likely after conditioning. Let’s verify with q=1: P(M_opens_B) = (1+1)/3 = 2/3. Posterior: P(A_prize | M_opens_B) = q/(q+1)=1/2. P(C_prize | M_opens_B) = 1/(q+1)=1/2. So switching is 1/2. But notice: if q=1, then if Monty opens C instead (rare under A_prize, impossible under C_prize), that would be extremely informative. In fact if q=1 and you observe Monty opened C, then it forces B_prize with probability 1, so switching would be 1. So the overall strategy that uses Monty’s behavior can do better than 2/3 in some cases, worse in others, but the unconditional always-switch strategy across all possible openings is still 2/3.
How can unconditional still be 2/3 if sometimes switching is 1/2?

Because the “sometimes” depends on which door he opens, and those outcomes occur with different probabilities. When switching is weaker in one observed branch, it’s stronger in the other branch, balancing out. We can compute expected win probability of “always switch” without conditioning on which door he opened. It’s simply P(initial choice is wrong) = 2/3, because Monty’s action doesn’t change whether your initial pick was correct. More explicitly: Always-switch wins if prize is not behind A. That’s P(B_prize or C_prize) = 2/3. This doesn’t depend on q. The conditional probabilities by branch can vary, but the overall average stays 2/3.
I still feel like after a goat is shown, there are two possible positions for the prize among two doors. Doesn’t that mean equiprobable?

“Two possible positions” does not imply “equiprobable.” Equiprobable requires symmetry or equal prior weights and equal likelihoods given the evidence. Here the evidence (Monty’s door opening) is not equally likely under the two hypotheses “prize behind your door” vs “prize behind the other unopened door.” That’s exactly what Bayes captured: the likelihood term P(M_opens_B | hypothesis) differs. To make this concrete, compare: Hypothesis H1: prize behind your door A. Hypothesis H2: prize behind remaining unopened door C. Given observation O: “Monty opened B,” - P(O | H1) = q (maybe 1/2 in classic) - P(O | H2) = 1 (Monty must open B if prize behind C) If P(O|H2) > P(O|H1), then O favors H2 over H1. That’s why not 1/2.
Give me an intuitive example that doesn’t rely on formulas. Something like a frequency argument.

Imagine repeating the game many times with the standard symmetric rules (q=1/2). You always pick A (just as a fixed habit), and the prize is placed uniformly at random. In 300 games: - About 100 times, prize is behind A. Monty opens B or C (goat), you switch and lose. - About 100 times, prize is behind B. Monty must open C, you switch to B and win. - About 100 times, prize is behind C. Monty must open B, you switch to C and win. So you win about 200 out of 300 by switching. If you stay, you win only in the first category: about 100 out of 300. The “two doors left” moment happens in all games, but the long-run frequencies show switching wins twice as often.
But in your 300 games, after Monty opens a door, sometimes he opens B and sometimes C. Doesn’t that mixture hide the true conditional probabilities?

Let’s split by what Monty opened, still in frequencies. Assume q=1/2 and you always pick A. Prize behind A (100 games): Monty opens B in ~50, opens C in ~50. Prize behind B (100 games): Monty opens C in 100. Prize behind C (100 games): Monty opens B in 100. Now count cases where Monty opens B: - From prize behind A: ~50 - From prize behind C: 100 Total ~150 games with Monty opens B. In those ~150 games, prize behind A occurs ~50 times, prize behind C occurs 100 times. So conditional on “Monty opened B,” probability prize behind C is 100/150 = 2/3. Similarly if Monty opens C, prize behind B is 100/150 = 2/3. So even conditioning on which door he opened, under q=1/2 you still get 2/3.
Suppose we change it: Monty doesn’t always offer the switch. He only offers switching sometimes. Does the 2/3 still hold?

Not necessarily. If Monty’s decision to offer a switch depends on where the prize is or on your initial choice, then the fact that he offered a switch becomes additional information, and the posterior can change. Classic problem assumes: Monty always offers a switch after opening a goat. If instead Monty offers a switch only when you initially picked the prize (or only when you didn’t), that could invert the advantage. So you must include “Monty always offers the option” as part of the information structure. If you observe the offer and it’s not guaranteed, you need to model it.
Fine. Back to classic. Another thing: why doesn’t Monty’s action “transfer” probability from the opened door equally to both remaining doors?

Because probability isn’t a fluid that must split equally when an option is removed. When an option is removed with a biased rule, the redistribution is not uniform. If a door is removed uniformly at random regardless of prize location, then yes, the remaining doors might become symmetric. But Monty’s removal is conditional: he removes a door specifically chosen to avoid the prize. That selection mechanism concentrates probability on the survivor among the unchosen doors. A helpful mental model: The 2/3 probability that your initial choice was wrong doesn’t disappear. Monty’s reveal doesn’t make your initial choice more likely to be correct; it just identifies which of the other two doors “inherits” the 2/3 mass.
That “inherits” phrasing seems like rhetoric. Can you justify it without metaphor?

Sure. Let X be the event “your initial pick is wrong.” P(X)=2/3. If your initial pick is wrong, the prize is in the set of doors you did not pick (two doors). Monty then opens a goat among those two, leaving exactly one unopened door in that set. Under the rules, if X occurs, the remaining unopened door in the unchosen set must be the prize door. So: P(switch wins) = P(X and remaining unopened door is prize) = P(X) = 2/3. No metaphor: it’s a direct logical implication: If initial pick wrong ⇒ switching wins (given Monty’s constrained action). If initial pick right ⇒ switching loses. So switching is equivalent to betting that your initial pick was wrong, which happens with probability 2/3.
But is it really true that if my initial pick is wrong, switching must win? What if Monty’s choice of which door to open affects that?

If your initial pick is wrong, then among the two unchosen doors, one is prize and one is goat. Monty is required to open a goat door. So he has no choice: he must open the goat, leaving the prize unopened. Therefore switching (to the only other unopened door) wins with certainty in that situation. Monty’s choice only matters when your initial pick is right, because then both unchosen doors are goats.
Okay. Let’s generalize to N doors. I’ve heard that with 100 doors switching is obviously better. But why is it “obvious” there and not for 3?

For N doors, the structure is the same. Rules: - N doors, 1 prize, N−1 goats. - You pick 1 door. - Host knows the prize location and opens N−2 doors among the unchosen doors, all goats, leaving exactly 2 unopened doors: your original pick and one other. - You can switch to the other unopened door. Probability your initial pick is correct: 1/N. Probability it’s wrong: (N−1)/N. If you switch: - If initial pick correct (1/N), switching loses. - If initial pick wrong ((N−1)/N), the host’s mass opening of goats leaves the single remaining unchosen door as the prize, so switching wins. Thus switching wins with probability (N−1)/N, which for N=3 is 2/3, and for N=100 is 99/100. It feels more obvious for large N because you strongly expect your initial pick to be wrong, and the host’s action essentially points you to the prize among the many doors you didn’t pick.

But with N doors, isn’t the host giving you a lot more information? He’s opening N−2 doors. In the 3-door version he opens only 1. Maybe the information content is what matters, not the initial probabilities.

The information content is indeed what converts the “wrong with probability (N−1)/N” into a sure win upon switching. But the initial probability already tells you how likely you are to be wrong. The host’s information is structured to preserve that. In both cases, the host’s action does not give you any reason to revise upward the chance your original door is correct, because the host was forced to avoid the prize. So the initial probability of being correct (1/N) stays the probability your original door is correct after the reveal (under the standard assumptions). The “amount of information” increases with N, but the core asymmetry—your initial choice vs the filtered alternative—exists already at N=3.
I want to test the claim that the probability of my original door remains 1/3 after Monty opens a goat. How can it remain the same when I learned something?

You learned something, but what you learned is mostly about the doors you didn’t pick, not about your chosen door. Learning “Monty opened a goat door he was forced to open” doesn’t change the chance your door was initially correct, because Monty would always be able to open a goat door regardless of whether your door was correct. The observation is compatible with both “your door is correct” and “your door is wrong,” but it’s more likely under “your door is wrong” in the symmetric case, which is why the other door becomes more likely. In Bayes terms, the posterior of your door can remain equal to its prior if the evidence has equal likelihood under “your door is correct” across the relevant set of observations. In the classic case, when you condition only on “Monty opened some goat door,” that event has probability 1 regardless of whether your door is correct, so it doesn’t update your door’s probability. When you condition on the specific door he opened, the likelihood can differ, but in the symmetric tie-break case it still works out to 1/3 for your door.
That sounds contradictory: you said conditioning on which door he opened can matter. But now you say it stays 1/3.

It depends on Monty’s tie-break behavior. - If Monty chooses randomly between the two goats when he has a choice (q=1/2), then conditioning on which door he opened still yields P(your door) = 1/3. - If Monty has a bias (q≠1/2) and you know it, then conditioning on which door he opened changes P(your door) to q/(q+1) or (1−q)/(2−q) depending on which door he opened. So there’s no contradiction: the invariance of 1/3 is not universal; it holds under the symmetric (or specified) Monty strategy. The classic textbook version typically assumes symmetry.
But the original TV show—did Monty actually choose randomly? If not, then the “2/3” might be wrong in reality.

In real-world versions, you’d need empirical data about host behavior: whether the host always offers a switch, whether the host always opens a goat, whether the host’s choice is random, etc. Without that, you can’t assign a unique numerical posterior. However, the standard Monty Hall problem is a mathematical puzzle with a specified host strategy (or at least specified constraints). Under those constraints, the solution is 2/3. If you change the constraints, you change the problem.

Let’s stay in the abstract. Another objection: If I decide to switch before anything happens, I’m committing to switching regardless of which door Monty opens. But once I see which door he opens, I could decide then. Why does committing early matter?

If Monty’s behavior is symmetric and independent of your future decision, committing early doesn’t matter for expected value: “always switch” is optimal and yields 2/3. If Monty’s behavior could depend on your apparent intentions, then committing early might matter, but that’s a different game (a strategic interaction). In the standard puzzle, Monty’s behavior is fixed by the rules; your decision can be made after the reveal without changing the probabilities, because you can still implement “always switch” after seeing the reveal.
Suppose I use a mixed strategy: sometimes I switch, sometimes I stay. What’s the best?

Under the classic assumptions (3 doors, Monty always opens a goat, tie-break doesn’t matter for unconditional), staying wins with probability 1/3, switching wins with probability 2/3. A mixed strategy that switches with probability s wins with probability: P(win) = s*(2/3) + (1−s)*(1/3) = (1/3) + s*(1/3). This is maximized at s=1, i.e., always switch.
Give me a full probability tree for the 3-door symmetric case. I want every branch labeled.

Sure. You pick A. Stage 1: prize location (each 1/3): 1) Prize A (1/3) 2) Prize B (1/3) 3) Prize C (1/3) Stage 2: Monty opens a door (goat), with symmetric tie-break: 1) Prize A (1/3): - Monty opens B (1/2) → outcome node prob 1/6 - Monty opens C (1/2) → outcome node prob 1/6 2) Prize B (1/3): - Monty must open C (1) → node prob 1/3 3) Prize C (1/3): - Monty must open B (1) → node prob 1/3 Now add final decision: If you stay with A: - Win only if prize A, regardless of which door opened. So win prob = 1/3. If you switch: - If Monty opened B, you switch to C. - If Monty opened C, you switch to B. Compute wins by branch: Prize A branches: switching loses (both 1/6 + 1/6 total 1/3 lose) Prize B branch: Monty opened C, switching to B wins (1/3 win) Prize C branch: Monty opened B, switching to C wins (1/3 win) Total switch win = 2/3.
You’re relying on “you pick A” without loss of generality. Is that safe? Doesn’t the choice matter?

It’s safe because before any doors are opened, the doors are symmetric. Your initial choice is not based on information, so whatever door you pick has probability 1/3 of being the prize. Labeling it A is just renaming. If you had some information that made one door more likely, then yes, the choice would matter, but that’s not the standard puzzle.
I want to see an argument that doesn’t involve renaming doors or symmetry. Something like: define random variables and compute.

Let’s define: - Prize location random variable P ∈ {1,2,3}, uniform. - Your initial choice random variable C ∈ {1,2,3}, independent of P, uniform (or fixed; either way symmetry holds). - Monty’s opened door random variable M ∈ {1,2,3} with constraints: M ≠ C, and M ≠ P (Monty never opens chosen door or prize door). If there are two possible doors satisfying constraints (when C=P), Monty selects among them uniformly. We want P(P = C | observed M). But we can compute P(P=C | M=m, C=c). Fix c and m with m≠c. We compute: P(P=c | M=m, C=c). By Bayes: P(P=c | M=m, C=c) ∝ P(M=m | P=c, C=c) P(P=c | C=c). Since P independent of C, P(P=c|C=c)=1/3. Now likelihood: If P=c, then Monty has two choices among the other two doors; probability he opens m is 1/2. If P≠c, then Monty has only one choice (the goat door among the two unchosen), so probability he opens m is either 1 or 0 depending on whether m is forced. Carrying out the normalization yields 1/3 for P=c and 2/3 for the remaining unopened door. This is essentially the earlier Bayes computation but in random-variable language.

I’m going to propose a different reasoning: At the moment after Monty opens a goat, I can think of the game as: one door is mine, one door is the other. I have no reason to prefer mine over the other, so it’s 1/2. Where is the flaw?

The flaw is “I have no reason to prefer mine over the other.” You do have a reason: yours was selected without information; the other was selected by a process that used information (Monty’s knowledge) to avoid the prize. That breaks the symmetry. More concretely, “mine” and “other” are not generated by the same procedure: - “mine” = your initial random pick. - “other” = the unique door among the two you didn’t pick that Monty did not open, where Monty’s opening avoided the prize. Different selection procedure ⇒ different probability distribution.
But Monty isn’t selecting the “other” door; he’s selecting the door to open. The other door is just what’s left. Does that matter?

It doesn’t change the logic. Selecting one door to open under a constraint is equivalent to selecting which door to leave closed under the complementary constraint. When your initial pick is wrong, Monty has no freedom: he must open the only goat among the unchosen pair, leaving the prize closed. When your initial pick is right, Monty has freedom: he leaves one of two goats closed. That asymmetry is exactly why the remaining unchosen closed door is more likely to be the prize.
If Monty’s reveal is informative, why doesn’t it increase the probability my initial door is correct sometimes?

It can in biased variants when conditioning on which door he opened, as we discussed. But in the symmetric classic case, it doesn’t because Monty’s action is designed to always be possible regardless of whether you initially picked correctly. The evidence mainly shifts probability among the unchosen doors: the opened door’s probability goes to zero, and its mass gets assigned to the remaining unchosen door, not to your chosen door. Your chosen door’s probability doesn’t need to increase because nothing about Monty’s action makes “you were right” more plausible relative to “you were wrong” in the symmetric setting.
Okay, let’s do a simulation argument, but purely conceptually. Describe an algorithm that would empirically show 2/3.

Algorithm: Repeat many trials: 1) Randomly place prize behind one of three doors uniformly. 2) Player picks a door uniformly (or always door 1). 3) Host opens a goat door among the two unpicked doors: - If player picked prize, host randomly chooses one of the two goat doors to open. - If player picked a goat, host must open the other goat door. 4) Player switches to the remaining unopened door. 5) Record whether player wins. After T trials, compute win rate. As T grows, win rate approaches 2/3. If instead player stays, win rate approaches 1/3.
Suppose I change step 3: if player picked prize, host opens the left goat; if player picked goat, host still opens a goat (forced). Does switching still give 2/3?

Unconditionally yes: always-switch still wins with probability 2/3 because it still wins exactly when initial pick is wrong. Conditioned on which door opened, the posterior changes (because host is deterministic when you picked prize). But the overall always-switch win probability remains 2/3.
Can you show that “wins exactly when initial pick is wrong” is always true regardless of host tie-breaking?

Yes, given the constraints “host never opens prize” and “host never opens your chosen door” and “host opens exactly one door leaving exactly two unopened doors.” If your initial pick is wrong, then the prize is among the two unchosen doors. Host must open a goat among those, so he must open the only goat there, leaving the prize as the only unopened unchosen door. Switching goes to that door, so you win. If your initial pick is correct, the two unchosen doors are both goats; host opens one goat, leaving one goat closed. Switching goes to a goat, you lose. So switching outcome is perfectly anti-correlated with correctness of initial pick. That’s independent of tie-breaking.
Here’s another challenge: what if Monty sometimes opens a goat even if it’s behind your chosen door? Like he could open your door?

That violates a key rule. If Monty can open your chosen door, then the structure changes. The “remaining unopened door” might not be uniquely determined, and the host’s action could reveal different information. You’d need a new model. The 2/3 result is specifically for the rule that Monty never opens your chosen door and always opens an unchosen goat.
Fine. I want to craft a “proof” that it’s 1/2 and you tell me exactly where it breaks.
Go ahead—state it carefully, step by step, and I’ll pinpoint the step that assumes something false.

Proof attempt: Initially each door has 1/3 chance. I pick one. Monty opens a goat door, so that opened door now has probability 0. The remaining probability 1 must be split among the two unopened doors. Since nothing distinguishes them now, each is 1/2. Therefore switching doesn’t matter.

The break is at “since nothing distinguishes them now.” Something does distinguish them: the process that led to one of them being unopened is different for your chosen door vs the other. More formally: after conditioning on Monty’s action, the two hypotheses “prize behind your door” and “prize behind the other door” do not have equal posterior because the likelihood of Monty’s action differs under those hypotheses. Your argument implicitly assumes that Monty’s action is independent of where the prize is (or at least equally likely under both hypotheses), which is false. Monty’s action is constrained by prize location, so the opened door being a goat is not a neutral observation.
But once the goat is revealed, it’s a fact. How can “likelihood of Monty’s action” matter after the fact?

Because probabilities are about your uncertainty given an information-generating process. Even after you observe a fact, you update beliefs based on how that fact would arise under different hidden states. Example: Suppose a machine outputs “Heads” with probability 0.9 if coin is biased type A, and 0.1 if coin is biased type B. After observing “Heads,” you should favor type A, because “Heads” is more likely under A than B. The fact is observed; the likelihood still matters for inference. Here, Monty opening a particular door (or just opening a goat) is more likely under some prize locations than others, so it shifts probabilities.
In the classic, Monty always opens a goat. So “Monty opened a goat” has likelihood 1 under all prize locations. Doesn’t that mean it gives no information?

If you condition only on the coarse event “Monty opened a goat door,” then yes, that event has probability 1 under all prize locations, so it gives no information. But you always knew Monty would do that under the rules; it’s not new. The informative part is which door remains unopened besides yours (equivalently which door Monty chose to open). Even if Monty always opens a goat, the identity of the opened door can be informative. In the symmetric classic case, that information doesn’t change the probability your door is correct (still 1/3), but it does determine which other door gets the 2/3.
But you just said conditioning only on “opened a goat” gives no info. Yet switching still helps. How can switching help if you didn’t gain information?

Because the advantage of switching doesn’t require you to gain new information about whether your initial choice is correct. The advantage is baked in from the start: your initial choice has probability 1/3 of being correct, and switching is designed to win when your initial choice is wrong. Think of it this way: switching is a strategy that exploits the host’s constraints, not a strategy that needs you to learn something about your chosen door. Even if you learned nothing, committing to “switch after the host opens a goat” is equivalent to choosing the set of two doors you didn’t initially pick, and then letting Monty’s forced reveal identify which of those two is the prize.
That sounds like another metaphor. Explain “equivalent to choosing the set of two doors.”

When you initially pick one door, you are implicitly rejecting the other two doors as a group. If you later switch after Monty opens one of those two, you end up with the remaining door from that group. So the “always switch” strategy can be seen as: “I bet that my initial pick is wrong, so I’m really betting on the other two doors.” Since the other two doors collectively have probability 2/3 of containing the prize, switching effectively captures that 2/3. Monty’s opening is the mechanism that collapses the two-door group into a single door without changing the group’s total probability.
But if I’m “betting on the other two doors,” why can’t I just pick two doors at the start? That’s not allowed.

Exactly—switching is a way to effectively obtain the benefit of having initially picked two doors, because Monty’s action removes one losing door from that two-door set for you. You can’t literally pick two at the start, but the host’s reveal gives you a second chance that functions similarly.

Let’s do an extreme variant: N doors, you pick 1, host opens N-2 goats, leaving 2 doors. If I stay, I have 1/N. If I switch, I have (N-1)/N. That seems clear. But for N=3, maybe small numbers behave differently.
They don’t; it’s the same formula. N=3 gives (3-1)/3 = 2/3. Small numbers can make intuition less stable, but the logic doesn’t change.
I want to see the general Bayes formula for N doors. Do it.

Let N doors. You pick door 1. Prize uniformly random among {1,...,N}. Host opens N−2 doors among {2,...,N} that are goats, leaving exactly one unchosen unopened door, call it door k (k ≠ 1). Assume when host has a choice (i.e., when prize behind door 1), he selects which door to leave unopened uniformly among {2,...,N}. We observe that the remaining unopened unchosen door is k. Compute P(prize behind 1 | remaining door is k). Let H be event “host leaves door k unopened” (equivalently opens all others). Compute: P(P=1 | H) = P(H | P=1)P(P=1) / P(H). P(P=1)=1/N. If P=1, host chooses which door to leave unopened among N−1 doors uniformly, so P(H|P=1)=1/(N−1). If P=k, then host must leave k unopened (since it has prize) and open all others goats, so P(H|P=k)=1. If P is some other door j ≠ 1,k, then host cannot leave k unopened while also not opening prize? Actually if prize behind j, host must leave j unopened, not k. So P(H|P=j)=0 for j≠1,k. Now: P(H) = P(H|P=1)P(P=1) + P(H|P=k)P(P=k) + sum_{j≠1,k} 0 = (1/(N−1))(1/N) + (1)(1/N) = 1/(N(N−1)) + 1/N = (1 + (N−1))/ (N(N−1)) = N/(N(N−1)) = 1/(N−1). Then: P(P=1 | H) = [(1/(N−1))(1/N)] / (1/(N−1)) = 1/N. So your door remains 1/N; the other remaining door has probability 1−1/N = (N−1)/N.
Okay, that’s solid. But I’m still bothered: why does your door remain 1/N? The observation H seems like it should affect it.

In that model, P(H|P=1)=1/(N−1) and P(H|P≠1) is either 1 or 0. The normalization works out so that P=1 stays 1/N. Intuitively, the host’s choice among the N−1 possible doors to leave unopened when you’re correct exactly offsets the fact that H is guaranteed when the prize is behind k. That’s the mechanism: when you’re correct, the host’s action is “less constrained” (many possible H outcomes), so any particular H is relatively unlikely under P=1. When you’re wrong in the specific way P=k, H is forced, so it’s more likely. That’s why H favors P=k and not P=1.
What if the host doesn’t choose uniformly among N−1 doors when you’re correct? Then does your door still stay 1/N?

Not necessarily when conditioning on which door remains. In general, the posterior can depend on the host’s selection probabilities among the doors he could leave. But the unconditional always-switch win probability remains (N−1)/N as long as the host always opens N−2 goats and always offers the switch, because switching wins iff your initial pick is wrong. Conditional posteriors can differ if the host’s “leave unopened” distribution is biased and known.
So the classic “2/3” is not a universal truth, it’s a statement about a specific host strategy.

It’s a statement about a specific set of rules. The minimal rules needed for the unconditional always-switch probability to be 2/3 are: (i) prize uniformly random, (ii) you choose randomly or without information, (iii) host always opens a goat door among unchosen doors and always offers a switch. That’s enough for “always switch” to win with 2/3, regardless of tie-breaking. If you want the stronger statement “after observing which door Monty opened, the remaining unopened door has probability 2/3,” then you need the additional assumption of symmetric tie-breaking (or at least that Monty’s choice doesn’t convey extra bias you can exploit).
Let’s try to create a variant where switching is worse than staying, but still Monty opens a goat and offers switching. Is that possible?

If Monty always opens a goat and always offers switching, and the prize placement is independent of your choice, then switching cannot be worse than staying in the 3-door setup: staying is 1/3, switching is 2/3 unconditionally. To make switching worse, you must violate something: perhaps Monty’s behavior depends on where the prize is in a way that changes which situations lead to an offer, or he sometimes opens the prize (and the game ends), or prize placement isn’t uniform, or your initial choice is informed. If you keep the standard constraints, switching is better.
But earlier you showed that conditional on Monty opening a particular door, switching could be as low as 1/2. That is worse than 2/3, but still better than 1/3. So okay.

Right: conditional can vary between 1/2 and 1 (in the 3-door biased-tie-break scenario), but it won’t drop below 1/2 because observing Monty open one door rules it out and leaves two possibilities, and with Monty constrained not to open the prize, the remaining unchosen door can’t become less likely than your chosen door by too much under those constraints. But the exact bound depends on Monty model. Unconditionally, the always-switch strategy stays at 2/3.
I’ve seen people argue using “information theory” that Monty’s action gives you a bit of information, and that’s why switching helps. Is that a correct viewpoint?

It can be framed that way, but it’s easy to make it sound mystical. The clean statement is: Monty’s action is correlated with the prize location, so it provides information. That correlation arises because Monty avoids opening the prize. In the N-door case, Monty’s action can reveal a lot (opening many goats), and switching captures the benefit. But you don’t need information theory to solve it; conditional probability is enough.
I want to see an explicit enumeration of all possible (prize, choice, Monty open) triples and count equally likely outcomes. Can you do that?

Yes, for the symmetric 3-door case where you pick A and Monty chooses randomly when he has a choice. Possible prize locations: A,B,C (each 1/3). For each, possible Monty open: - Prize A: - Monty opens B with prob 1/2 - Monty opens C with prob 1/2 - Prize B: - Monty opens C with prob 1 - Prize C: - Monty opens B with prob 1 So the four outcome states with their probabilities: 1) (Prize A, Monty opens B): 1/6 2) (Prize A, Monty opens C): 1/6 3) (Prize B, Monty opens C): 1/3 4) (Prize C, Monty opens B): 1/3 If Monty opened B, that corresponds to states 1 and 4 with total probability 1/2. In that conditional space: - Prize A occurs with prob (1/6)/(1/2)=1/3 - Prize C occurs with prob (1/3)/(1/2)=2/3 Similarly for Monty opened C.
If we instead assume that Monty chooses which door to open uniformly among the two unchosen doors, without regard to whether it’s the prize, what happens?

Then it’s a different problem. In that variant, sometimes Monty reveals the prize immediately, ending the game. Conditional on Monty opening a goat, the probabilities can become 1/2. Let’s compute quickly. You pick A. Prize equally likely. Monty opens B or C uniformly at random (1/2 each), regardless of prize. Now observe Monty opened B and it’s a goat. That observation rules out prize behind B. Remaining possibilities: prize behind A or C. Are they equal? Yes, because Monty’s choice was independent of prize, and the event “opened B” has equal likelihood under A_prize and C_prize (both 1/2). Thus posterior is 1/2 each. So switching is 1/2 in that conditional scenario. This is the common source of confusion: people unconsciously assume Monty could have opened the prize but happened not to.
So the key is: Monty’s knowledge and constraint changes everything.
Exactly. The host is not just removing a random door; he’s removing a door chosen to avoid the prize. That selection bias is the entire puzzle.
Can you phrase the problem in terms of conditional independence? Like, which variables are independent and which aren’t?

Yes. Let P = prize location, C = your initial choice, M = Monty’s opened door. In the classic model: - P is independent of C (you choose without information). - M is not independent of P given C, because Monty’s choice depends on where the prize is relative to your choice. - In fact, given C, M is a deterministic function of P in the cases where C ≠ P (host forced), and is random only when C=P. The mistake that leads to 1/2 often assumes M is independent of P given C (or independent outright), which is false.

I think I get it. But I want you to address a subtlety: After Monty opens a goat, isn’t the remaining unopened door also “chosen without information” because Monty might have had a choice and could have left either closed?

The remaining unopened door is not “chosen without information” in the relevant sense. Even if Monty had a choice when you were correct, when you were wrong he had no choice: he was forced to leave the prize door closed. So the distribution of the remaining unopened door depends on whether you were correct. That dependence is the information. When you’re correct (1/3), the remaining unopened door is a random goat among two goats. When you’re wrong (2/3), the remaining unopened door is the prize with certainty. So overall, the remaining unopened door is much more likely to be the prize.
Let’s do a different approach: compute expected value of staying vs switching using indicator variables.

Define indicator I = 1 if your initial pick is correct, 0 otherwise. Then P(I=1)=1/3. If you stay, you win iff I=1, so win probability = E[I] = 1/3. If you switch, you win iff I=0, so win probability = E[1−I] = 1−E[I] = 2/3. That’s compact and doesn’t reference Monty’s tie-breaking because it uses only the logical fact that switching flips the outcome relative to initial correctness (under the classic constraints).
But that “logical fact” uses Monty’s constraints again. Okay.
Right: the equivalence “switch wins iff initial pick wrong” is where the host constraints enter.
I want to see a counterexample where Monty opens a goat but switching is not equal to 1 − P(initial correct). Is that possible?

Yes, if Monty’s constraints are weaker, e.g., he might open a goat but not necessarily leave exactly one unopened unchosen door, or he might sometimes open a goat but sometimes open the prize and then reclose it (weird), or he might open a goat but not guarantee offering you the remaining unopened door, etc. But with the standard constraint “Monty opens exactly one unchosen goat and leaves exactly one other unchosen door unopened,” then switching is exactly the complement of initial correctness.
Let’s talk about the “principle of indifference.” People apply it to say the two remaining doors are equally likely. Why is that principle misapplied?

The principle of indifference applies when you have no reason to assign different probabilities because the states are symmetric with respect to your information and the generative process. Here, the two remaining doors are not symmetric relative to the generative process: - Your door was chosen by you without information. - The other remaining door was selected (left unopened) by a process that depends on the prize location. So indifference doesn’t apply.
Could you formalize “symmetry” here? Like a permutation argument?

Yes. Consider permutations of door labels. Before any actions, the joint distribution is invariant under permutations: each door is equally likely to hide the prize. After you pick a specific door (say A), symmetry is broken: A is now special as “chosen.” The distribution remains symmetric among the unchosen doors B and C. Then Monty opens one unchosen door with a rule that treats B and C symmetrically when he has a choice (in the classic). That preserves symmetry between B and C in the “prize behind A” case, but not between A and the remaining unchosen door, because A was chosen without information while the remaining door is conditioned on Monty’s action. So the only symmetry left after Monty acts is not “A vs remaining,” but rather “if Monty opened B then remaining is C; if Monty opened C then remaining is B” with equal structure.
I’ve also heard an argument: “Switching doubles your chances.” Is that literally true or just approximate?

In the 3-door classic, yes: switching probability 2/3 is exactly double staying probability 1/3. In N-door generalization, switching is (N−1)/N and staying is 1/N, so switching is (N−1) times better, not double.
If I want to explain this to someone skeptical like me, what’s the single most convincing short argument?

The “initial choice is wrong with probability 2/3, and if it’s wrong then switching must win” argument. More explicitly: - You have 1/3 chance you picked the prize. - You have 2/3 chance you picked a goat. - Monty’s action guarantees that if you picked a goat, the remaining unopened door is the prize. So switching wins with probability 2/3.
But that still feels like it ignores the fact that Monty opens a goat. Like it’s too quick.

It uses the fact implicitly: the guarantee that if you picked a goat then Monty will open the other goat, leaving the prize. If Monty could open the prize, or might not open a door, the argument fails. So it’s not ignoring it; it’s leveraging the constraint.
Okay. I’ll ask something else: if you’re allowed to choose after Monty opens, could you ever do better than 2/3 by using which door he opened?

Under classic symmetric tie-breaking, no: whichever door he opens, the remaining unopened unchosen door has probability 2/3, so always switching is optimal and gives 2/3. Under biased tie-breaking that you know, yes: you might condition your choice on which door he opened to do better than 2/3 in some cases, but if the bias is known you can still do at least 2/3 on average by always switching; you might improve by sometimes not switching depending on the signal. But note: if Monty’s bias is only in the case you were correct (C=P), then the posterior after observing which door opened can shift your belief about whether you were correct. Then a strategy that sometimes stays could beat always-switch in that model.
Can you construct an explicit example where a conditional strategy beats always switching?

Yes. Let Monty’s tie-break be extremely biased: if you picked the prize, he opens B with probability 1 (q=1), opens C with probability 0. If you picked a goat, he’s forced. You pick A. If Monty opens C, that can only happen if prize is behind B (because if prize A, he would open B; if prize C, he can’t open C). So after observing Monty opened C, switching to B wins with probability 1. If Monty opens B, posterior is 1/2 that prize is A and 1/2 that prize is C, so switching to C wins with probability 1/2. A strategy: switch if Monty opens C (win prob 1). If Monty opens B, you might be indifferent: stay gives 1/2, switch gives 1/2. So no improvement over always-switch in expectation here; it remains 2/3 overall. To beat 2/3, you’d need a model where Monty’s behavior when you are wrong is also non-forced in a way that encodes extra information, or where he sometimes deviates from “always offer switch” depending on state. Under the strict “he must open a goat” and only has freedom when you’re correct, the best you can do overall is still 2/3, though you can get conditional certainty in some branches.
Interesting. So despite bias, you can’t beat 2/3 overall if all else is standard?

If the only nontrivial choice Monty has is when you’re correct, and he always opens a goat and always offers switching, then the overall probability your initial pick is wrong remains 2/3, and switching capitalizes on that. Conditional strategies might not increase the overall success beyond 2/3 because the only time you’d want to stay is when you think you were correct, but those situations remain only 1/3 in total prior mass, and Monty’s signal is limited. But with more elaborate host policies (e.g., sometimes he opens a door only in certain states), you can get different numbers.
I think I’m convinced for the classic puzzle now. But I want to ensure I can explain to someone why “two doors left implies 1/2” is wrong. Give a crisp rebuttal.

“Two doors left” only implies 1/2 if the two remaining possibilities are equally likely given the process that produced the observation. Here, the host’s action depends on the prize location (he avoids opening it), so the observation is more likely when the prize is behind the other door than when it’s behind yours. Therefore the two doors are not equally likely; switching is 2/3.
Good. Now, can we end with a compact summary using equations only?

Let C be your chosen door, P the prize door, and S the event “host opens a goat door and offers switch” (assumed always). Then: P(P=C)=1/3. Switch wins iff P≠C, so: P(switch wins)=P(P≠C)=1−P(P=C)=2/3. P(stay wins)=P(P=C)=1/3. If conditioning on a specific opened door with symmetric tie-break, then for remaining unopened door R: P(P=R | observation)=2/3, P(P=C | observation)=1/3.
All right. I concede. Switching is 2/3 in the standard setup.
Why is it that integration and differentiation are inverses? It feels like a coincidence that area under a curve and slope of a tangent are related.

It stops feeling like a coincidence once you notice both operations are built from the same limiting process, but applied in opposite “directions.” Differentiation takes a function and asks: if I nudge the input by a tiny amount h, how much does the output change, per unit h, in the limit as h→0? That’s a local rate of change. Integration (in the Riemann sense) takes a function and asks: if I add up many tiny rectangles of width Δx and height f(x), what do I get in the limit as the widths go to 0? That’s an accumulation of local contributions. The key is that “accumulation of rates” reconstructs the net change, and “rate of accumulated change” recovers the original rate. That statement is not just poetic; it’s exactly what the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus (FTC) formalizes. There are two closely related parts: 1) If F(x) = ∫_a^x f(t) dt (area/accumulation up to x), then F′(x) = f(x).
